Contextual Factors
Please use this question to detail any contextual factors you wish to make the Selection Committee aware of.
NIHR wants to know about any circumstances so that they may take them into consideration during the
assessment of your application. Contextual factors may include:
 Career breaks due to parental leave, or periods of illness.
 Reduced time spent undertaking research due to a disability or c

secarb__001_g.text:
aring responsibilities. This could include
any physical or mental difficulty that may have impacted your research career. These are situations that
have a significant impact on your ability to undertake research.
 Reduced opportunities to career support e.g. mentorship, and limited opportunities to undertake prior
research and training.
 Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on your research career.
Please also use this section to detail any other factors that may have impacted your research career not listed in
the examples provided. The impact on your career to date will be specific to your particular circumstances but
could include such impacts as limited opportunities to obtain grant funding, or fewer publications. In general terms,
mitigating factors should be significant, and relevant.
NIHR acknowledges that you may be reluctant, or uncomfortable disclosing relevant information that is sensitive.
However, you should bear in mind that we are unable to take into account factors that you do not disclose. Please
be assured that information provided by you is sensitive and will be treated confidentially and in line with General
Data and Protection Regulations (GDPR).
There is one key

secarb__001_h.text:
mitigating factor that impacted my research career so far: the Covid-19 pandemic.
I joined University of Kent in September 2019. Six months later the first lockdown was enforced on 26th March
2020. This was followed by a series of lockdowns and restrictions until December 2021.
Since joining University of Kent, I have been working across several projects that include primary data collection in
adult social care. While data collection in adult social care is challenging and time demanding, this became a
particular issue during the COVID-19 pandemic. More specifically, adult social care was one of the most impacted
sectors during this time. This meant for example that:
1. Data collection was completely halted and delayed. This was due to, e.g. being unable to obtain research
governance approvals. Many local authorities temporarily suspended their research governance approval process.
Importantly local authorities and adult social care organisations were unable to support recruitment of participants.
2. Changes to data collection. As we experienced issues with data collection, we had to explore new recruitment
pathways: e.g. data collection through NHS instead of adult social care org

secarb__001_i.text:
anisations.
3. Data collection was time consuming: for example, to meet our target of recruiting 300 participants, I had to send
10000 individual emails to potentially eligible participants - this consumed the majority of my hours dedicated to
the project. In addition, I was responsible for preparing study packages and sending them to participants.
The above are a few of the examples of how the Covid-19 pandemic impacted projects I was involved in. The key
impact was that I spent much of my time on ensuring we would collect data on time and meet our recruitment
targets. Hence, I had no time to dedicate to writing either research papers or applying for funding applications.
Since 2022, I have been able to resume applying for research grants, leading on publications and leadership
positions within my organisation in addition to my everyday tasks.

secpesor__001_a.text:
Aim(s) of the research: The focus of this Fellowship is on family members and friends who support someone
living with dementia (unpaid carers). Eight former and current unpaid carers highlighted that they would like to
understand how they can increase their physical activity, improve their diet and manage their time better.
Therefore, as a first step, we will prioritise which concern out of these three is the most important for unpaid carers
living across England. As a second step, we will then design an intervention that will help unpaid carers to address
the concern selected in the first step. As a last step, we will test whether the developed intervention is practical
and acceptable for unpaid carers and for those who may be delivering it.
Background to the research: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends who support a
person living with dementia. In the academic literature they are referred to as carers, unpaid carers, family carers
or supporters etc. but the family members and friends may not use any of these terms to describe themselves. As
a result of their caring responsibilities, carers are often unable to do things that would support their own health a
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
nd
wellbeing. Unpaid carers themselves highlighted that ‘It is exhausting to be a carer, it takes so much out of you’.
Currently, we know very little about how we can support unpaid carers to increase their physical activity, improve
their diet and manage their time better.
Design and methods used: In this Fellowship we will follow a step by step guide called the Behaviour Change
Wheel to understand how we can support unpaid carers to improve their health and wellbeing. In Stage 1 we will
conduct a needs assessment (through reviewing existing evidence and collecting information among 100 unpaid
carers living in England) to identify whether we should focus on e.g. helping carers to manage their time better (to
have ‘me time’). In Stage 2 we will use a range of methods to design the intervention itself. This will include a
consensus exercise with 30 unpaid carers to define the exact focus of the intervention. We will then conduct five
focus groups (3-5 people per group) with unpaid carers to gain further insight into how the intervention should look.
We will then design the intervention itself and share it with five focus groups (3-5 people per group) including e.g.
carers, health an
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
d social care professionals etc. to gather their feedback. In Stage 3 we will test whether the
proposed intervention is practical and acceptable by the unpaid carers and the people who may deliver it.
Patient/service user, carer and public involvement: Unpaid carers played a central role in co-producing this
Fellowship application. Over a period of twelve months we met on a monthly basis to decide together the focus of
the Fellowship, how we are going to answer our research questions, disseminate results etc. We were a team of
ten people - eight people self-identified as a former or current unpaid carer living across England and Scotland.
They will continue to work on the Fellowship as co-researchers – this means they will be part of the core research
team, they will jointly own the research and be responsible for it.
Dissemination: Unpaid carers highlighted that it is important that the results from this Fellowship reach a wide
range of people including carers, health and social care professionals and that the results are shared throughout
the Fellowship regularly. We will use a range of approaches, for example: Podcast/YouTube channel; articles; and
working with an illustrator to
secpesor__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secsa__001_a.text:
Research question: What does a feasible and acceptable intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or
time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia look like?
Background: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends of people living with dementia who
are unpaid carers. While being a carer can be rewarding, caring is associated with a range of mental and/or
physical health difficulties. Based on the evidence gap in the literature and working together with eight current and
former unpaid carers, we identified a need to develop an intervention to improve unpaid carers’ physical activity,
diet and time management in order to support their health and wellbeing.
Aims and objectives: The ultimate goal of this Fellowship is to design and evaluate feasibility of a behaviour
change intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living
with dementia.
Methods: The study will use mixed methods across three stages of work. Eight unpaid carers co-produced the
suggested research and will be part of the core research team throughout the Fellowship. In Stage 1 (needs
assessment) we will conduct a survey

secsa__001_b.text:
(online or paper) with 100 unpaid carers of people living with dementia
across England to identify whether the focus of the intervention should be on physical activity and diet or time
management. This decision will also be supported by a systematic review to understand where the highest need
for an intervention development is. In Stage 2 we will develop the intervention itself focusing on key concern(s) as
identified in Stage 1, e.g. diet and physical activity. We will use a range of methods as recommended by the
Behaviour Change Wheel. First, we will conduct an online Delphi consensus exercise with 30 unpaid carers to
define target behaviours (e.g. healthy snacking; healthy cooking etc.). We will then conduct five focus groups (3-5
people per group) with unpaid carers to define the exact focus of the intervention including what needs to change
in the person and/or the environment in order to e.g. stop snacking on high-fat/salt/sugar snacks. Once we
understand what needs to change, the research team with the support of other experts will design the intervention
itself and conduct five focus groups (3-5 people per group) with e.g. unpaid carers, other family members, health
and soc

secsa__001_c.text:
ial care professionals etc. to gather feedback. In Stage 3 we will conduct a feasibility study to test whether
the proposed intervention is feasible and acceptable for unpaid carers and those who may deliver it.
Timelines for delivery: Stage 1: 1-12 months; Stage 2: 13-29 months; Stage 3: 30-44 months.
Anticipated impact and dissemination: A functional, feasible, and acceptable intervention targeting physical
activity, diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia across England. For
dissemination of results, we will use a range of approaches, for example: Podcast/YouTube channel; webinars;
articles for the Alzheimer's & Dementia Journal or the Journal of Dementia Care; working with an illustrator to
capture some of the findings etc.

secdrp__001_a.text:
What is the problem being addressed?
Dementia is fast becoming the defining health and social care issue of our time. It is estimated that there are
currently 944,000 people with dementia in the UK and this is likely to increase with an ageing population[2].
As dementia progresses, many people find it increasingly difficult to look after themselves and their homes and
may need help with daily activities[3]. Most of this help is provided by family, close friends or neighbours (also
referred to as unpaid, informal or family carers). In this Fellowship, I use the term ‘unpaid carers’, a term preferred
by eight former and current unpaid carers of people living with dementia (co-researchers) who co-produced this
application.
Unpaid carers support individuals with dementia in a range of ways, including personal care, cooking, finances,
emotional support and arranging support from services[4]. It is estimated that unpaid carers spend 1.1 billion hours
each year supporting people with dementia[5]. As highlighted by Fiona Carragher from the Alzheimer's Society:
‘Unpaid carers of people with dementia, are part of the backbone of the care sector, but these unsung heroes are
being pushed to br

secdrp__001_b.text:
eaking point’[6]. Importantly unpaid carers of people living with dementia highlighted while
working on this application that ‘Carers are overwhelmed as it is expected from them to do it all’ and ‘It is time to
recognise how the caring impacts family members and friends of those living with dementia’.
Between January 2023 and January 2024, I met monthly with eight unpaid former or current carers (co-
researchers) of people living with dementia who live across Scotland and England. They identified 22 health and
wellbeing concerns experienced by carers of people living with dementia, the most important ones being:
1.Exercise, 2. Diet, 3. Coping with stress, 4. Coping with terminal illness, 5. Coping with emotions, 6. Getting help,
7. Knowledge and 8. Managing their time better[1].
At the moment, very little is known about physical activity, time management and diet of unpaid carers of
people with dementia when compared to other concerns (copying with emotions and stress[7]; knowledge[8];
copying with terminal illness/grief[9] and getting help e.g. from adult social care services[10]).
Therefore, the focus of this Fellowship is on unpaid carers of people living with dementia in Englan

secdrp__001_c.text:
d and how
we can support them to improve both their physical activity and diet or to manage their time better.
Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or wellbeing of the public and/or to
patients/service users, carers and health and care services?
The total costs of dementia in the UK in 2019 amounted to £34.7 billion. Unpaid care account for 40% (£13.9
billion) of the total costs[11]. Without unpaid carers it would be financially impossible for health and adult social
care to provide the huge amount of unpaid care (e.g. up to 100 hours per week during pandemic[12]) provided by
carers[11].
While caring for someone living with dementia can be rewarding[13], unpaid carers of people living with dementia
experience a range of mental and physical health issues(e.g.[14-16]) including stress, depression, anxiety, grief
and physical and emotional burden. Unpaid carers have an increased mortality[17] and worse physical and mental
health outcomes especially among women, those looking after a spouse, and those providing very high levels of
care[18]. An Alzheimer’s Society survey in 2020 found that two-thirds of unpaid carers of people living with
dementia ‘despera

secdrp__001_d.text:
tely needed more support to protect their wellbeing’[6].
Review of existing evidence: how does the existing literature support this proposal?
Unpaid carers of people living with dementia experience a range of mental and physical health issues (e.g.
(e.g.[14-16]). Very little is known about physical activity, time management and diet of unpaid carers of
people with dementia when compared to other concerns (e.g. copying with emotions and stress[7]; knowledge
[8]; copying with terminal illness/grief[9] an getting help e.g. from adult social care services[10]).
No studies have explored how unpaid carers of people living with dementia can improve their time management.
The need to manage time better was identified by co-researchers themselves. Examples included: ‘I never get any
time for myself’; ‘I have no time to do shopping today’ and ‘I am so bad at managing my time’.
No studies have explored how unpaid carers of people living with dementia can improve their diet. Again, the need
to improve diet was identified by co-researchers. Examples included: neglecting own meals while focusing on
person with dementia; unhealthy snacking; poor quality ready meals; and binge eating.
A systematic

secdrp__001_e.text:
review of physical activity interventions for unpaid carers of people with dementia found that studies
were too methodologically weak to allow robust conclusions[19]. Methodological issues of the included studies
were also reported in another systematic review of exercise for caregiver–care recipient dyads[20].
What is the research question/aims and objectives?
The ultimate goal of this Fellowship is to design and evaluate the feasibility of a behaviour change intervention
targeting physical activity, diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia. Throughout
the Fellowship I will answer the following research question: What does a feasible and acceptable intervention
targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia in
England look like?
Project Plan
The proposed work is informed by Medical Research Council guidance for developing and evaluating complex
interventions[21]; the Behaviour Change Wheel[22]; and Co-production.
The study will use mixed methods across three stages of work (Figure 1. Research plan):
1. Stage 1 (needs assessment; 1-12 months)
2. Stage 2 (intervention development; 13-29 mot

secdrp__001_f.text:
hs)
3. Stage 3 (feasibility study; 30-44 months).
Equality, diversity and inclusion (EDI): information will be collected and results will be summarised where
possible based on unpaid carer’s ethnicity/race, age, gender and sexual orientation. We will ensure that we will
recruit participants from a range of backgrounds including recruiting participants from specific areas to sample
different ethnicities, socioeconomic backgrounds and levels of access to health and dementia care services. We
will also engage with organisations that specifically support LGBTQ+ communities. I am also a member of
Dementia Community EDI specialty interest group and will draw on the members’ expertise.
Examples of the organisations that we will engage with are: Opening Doors; Switchboard Dementia; LGBTQ+
Dementia Advisory Group team; The Ethnic Minorities in Canterbury (EMIC); Diversity Resource International; the
West Sussex Alzheimer’s Society (link established with chair of the Dementia Strategy Minorities Sub-group),
Carers Support West Sussex (link with Bi-lingual Dementia Navigator); and Meri Yaadain CiC (supporting ethnic
communities). I will also draw on the links of the Clinical Research Network

secdrp__001_g.text:
lead (Kent, Surrey and Sussex),
namely the Research Engagement Network that have connections with Somalis and South Sudanese
communities.
The length of each focus group will be up to one hour and times will be provided outside typical working hours.
Both online and in person focus groups will be offered. We will contribute to carers’ replacement costs so
participants can take part in research while a professional is supporting a person with dementia. If people do not
wish to participate in a focus group, e.g. due to a language barrier, an option will be provided to have an individual
interview with an interpreter present. All documents will be translated into three additional languages.
Stage 1: Needs assessment; 1-12 months: survey and systematic review
While there is no agreement on how many concerns an intervention should target, the intervention itself will only
focus on one of these concerns: 1. both physical activity and diet or 2. time management (e.g. prioritising time for
yourself). As in current practice it would be unusual in public health to address physical activity and diet
separately, if selected the plan is that the intervention itself will target both. However, as

secdrp__001_h.text:
part of the survey and
systematic review we will explore whether indeed the intervention should target both diet and physical activity or
whether the focus should be on one only.
Stage 1a: Needs assessment; 1-12 months: survey
Aim: to identify the top health and wellbeing concern of unpaid carers of people living with dementia. In addition,
we will aim to gather input on the key barriers and enablers to support unpaid carers to tackle these concerns;
mode of delivery of the intervention; and who should deliver it. This information will be then used in Stage 2 to
inform intervention development.
Study design: a self-administered questionnaire for unpaid carers in England. Participants will be able to choose
between a postal or online version (using Qualtrics XM Platform™). It is important to highlight that the James Lind
Alliance Priority Setting Partnership[23] was not considered as appropriate method for this study as we have
already done some of the ground work. For example, the proposed three health and wellbeing concerns were
suggested by eight unpaid carers themselves. However, as highlighted by co-researchers: ‘Everyone is different
and it is important to take time to evaluat

secdrp__001_i.text:
e people’s needs’. Therefore, there is a need to conduct a survey among
wider group of unpaid carers to identify the top health and wellbeing concern.
Survey: all study documents including survey will be developed together with eight co-researchers. The survey will
include questions about:
1. The Caregiving situation: former vs current carer, whether the carer and person with dementia live in the
same household, relationship to a person living with dementia, estimated hours of care per week, any
additional support received from other family members and friends, health care and adult social care
services etc.
2. Sociodemographic characteristics: age; gender; ethnicity; region; urban vs rural area; socioeconomic
status; English as a second language.
3. Carer quality of life of someone with dementia: a condition-specific measure of the quality of carers of
people with dementia[24].
4. Mode of data collection: paper or online.
5. Top health and wellbeing concern: able to choose from the three health and wellbeing concerns
suggested by co-researchers as described earlier. Participants will have an opportunity to suggest other
health and wellbeing concerns and will be provided also with

secdrp__001_j.text:
a free text box to explain their decision.
6. To inform the Intervention development: questions around mode of delivery of the intervention, who should
deliver it and what are the key barriers and enablers to support unpaid carers to tackle these concerns
(questions informed by both COM-B model[22] and the Theoretical Domains Framework (TDF)[25].
Participants and setting: unpaid carers of people living with dementia across England. Both former (within five
years[26]) and current carers will be eligible to take part, if they provide or provided unpaid support to a relative,
partner/spouse or friend with dementia. They will have to be at least 18 years old. While this survey is not James
Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership per se, I used their recommendation on sample size to determine how
many participants need to be recruited for this survey. At present, there is no recommended maximum or minimum
number of responses. Survey responses from previous priority settings have varied from 100s to 1,000s[23]. As
such we will aim to recruit at least 100 participants (maximum 500). We will distribute the survey through multiple
channels: GP practices, memory services, community pharma

secdrp__001_k.text:
cies, social media (e.g., Twitter), previous studies,
online dementia research websites (e.g. Join Dementia Research (JDR)), carer organisations and dementia
services. The recruitment will be opened up to four months.
Analysis: descriptive analysis (e.g. frequency, median). We will also explore whether there are any differences in
the selected top health and wellbeing concerns between e.g. former and current carers; carers from different
socioeconomic backgrounds etc. If we find differences, this will be discussed with the Steering Committee (SC)
and their advice will be sought on how to deal with this.
Outcome: top health and wellbeing concern of unpaid carers of people living with dementia. Information about
mode of delivery for the intervention, who should deliver it and any key barriers and facilitators. In addition, we will
establish the suitability of recruitment pathways and whether any changes to recruitment have to be introduced for
the Stage 2.
Stage 1b: Needs assessment; 1-12 months: systematic review and meta-analysis
Alongside the survey (described in Stage 1a), we will conduct a systematic review and meta-analysis.
Research question:
1. Are there effective interventio

secdrp__001_l.text:
ns to improve: physical activity; diet; and time management among unpaid
carers of people living with dementia and other conditions?
Study design: systematic review and meta-analysis. Protocol for this review will be registered a priori in the
PROSPERO database. The reporting will follow the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-
Analyses (PRISMA) guideline[27].
Eligibility criteria, information sources and search strategy: only studies published in English in peer-reviewed
journals will be included. We will only include randomised trials. As highlighted earlier there are limited studies
among unpaid carers of people living with dementia focusing on diet, physical activity and time management.
Therefore, I will broaden the inclusion criteria on other carers that may have similar experiences – this will be
guided by discussion with co-researchers, SC and by the number of studies identified. The outcomes of interest
will be levels of physical activity; diet; and time management. The search strategy will be developed in consultation
with university librarian and mentors. The titles, abstracts and full texts will be screened by at least two reviewers.
Risk of bias:

secdrp__001_m.text:
we will use Critical Appraisal Checklists[28] to assess the risk of bias in included studies.
Data synthesis and statistical analysis: the detailed statistical plan will be reviewed by a statistician. Where
possible I will combine data from different studies using random effects meta-analysis.
Outcome: comprehensive list of interventions including whether they work, in which setting they were tested and
with whom.
At the end of Stage 1, I will organise an SC meeting. The aim of this meeting will be to review the findings from
Stage 1 and decide which health and wellbeing concern will be taken forward to Stage 2. As suggested by Medical
Research Council guidance[21] we will consider the following criteria when making this decision: priority;
opportunity to intervene; ability to change; size of potential benefit; stakeholder desirability for an intervention etc.
Implementation challenges will be also considered.
Stage 2: Intervention development; 13-29 moths
Stage 2 will focus on development of an intervention for health and wellbeing concern as identified by the end of
Stage 1. The Intervention development will be informed by the Behaviour Change Wheel[22]. It includes following
ste

secdrp__001_n.text:
ps: a) Understanding the behaviour; b) Understanding what needs to change; c) Identifying intervention options;
and d) Identifying content and implementation options.
Stage 2a: Understanding the behaviour; 16-21 months: Delphi consensus exercise
Aim: to understand the behaviour that will be the target of the intervention.
Study design: we will conduct a Delphi consensus exercise as recommended by authors of Behaviour Change
Wheel with a series of rounds where stakeholders produce a list of target behaviours (‘What behaviour needs to
change?’) and actors (‘Who is involved?’). For example, as a result of Stage 1, we may agree that the focus of the
intervention should be improved diet. However, improved diet is not a behavioural target. Behavioural targets for
improved diet can include for example: buying groceries on the list; batch cooking and freezing portions ready to
eat; carrying healthy snacks etc. For ‘who is involved’ examples can include, e.g. unpaid carer; meal service
provider; friends etc. The aim of the Delphi exercise will be to generate all the target behaviours and actors (e.g.
unpaid carers; meal providers etc.).
Delphi consensus exercise: We will conduct a 2-round o

secdrp__001_o.text:
nline modified Delphi consensus exercise using
Qualtrics XM Platform™. The Delphi consensus exercise is designed to elicit a ‘consensus of opinion’ from a
group of experts. Participants remain anonymous and provide feedback as part of the iterative process[29]. The
aim of the Delphi consensus exercise will be to develop a consensus among unpaid carers of people living with
dementia on a list of target behaviours and actors. It will include background questions about the unpaid carer and
their sociodemographic characteristics (similar to Stage 1a). The initial list of questions, ‘What behaviour needs to
change?’ ‘Who is involved?’ ‘What makes it more or less likely to succeed?’, for the expert panel to consider were
already generated by eight co-researchers involved in this application. Examples for e.g. diet can be found here[1].
In addition, we will include suggestions as collected in Stage 1a (survey).
Participants and settings: unpaid carers of people living with dementia across England. Both former (within five
years[26]) and current carers will be eligible to take part, if they provide or provided unpaid support to a person
with dementia. They will have to be at least 18 years

secdrp__001_p.text:
old. It is important to note that there is no agreement on the
minimum panel size required for content validity in Delphi studies. Recommendations vary from 5-30
participants[30, 31]. Given the diversity of experiences in carers of people living with dementia, we will aim to
recruit up to 30 participants. We will include and build on our recruitment strategies as suggested in Stage 1a.
Participants taking part in Stage 1a will be also asked whether they would agree to be contacted in future about
research studies. The recruitment and data collection will be opened to four months. I acknowledge that those who
do not have access to internet/do not wish to engage in Delphi online exercise will be excluded from this study.
Analysis: descriptive statistics (e.g. median and frequencies) for representing the group opinion.
Outcome: core behaviours will be identified. We will understand: ‘What behaviour needs to change?’ ‘Who is
involved?’ ‘What makes it more or less likely to succeed?’
Once the Delphi exercise is finalised I will organise an SC meeting. Building on the results from the Delphi
consensus exercise we will make a final decision which target behaviours the intervention should

secdrp__001_q.text:
address using
the criteria of impact, likelihood of change, positive or negative spill-over to other behaviours and ease of
measurement of outcomes. We will ensure that this decision is culturally, socially and context driven and
acceptable. The target behaviours will be described in terms who needs to perform the behaviour, what the
persons need to do differently, when, where, how, and with whom will they do it.
Stage 2b: Specify what needs to change; 19 – 24 months: focus groups
The COM-B model suggests that changing the incidence of any behaviour of an individual, group or population
involves changing one or more of the following: capability (physical/psychological), opportunity (physical/social),
and motivation (reflective/automatic) relating either to the behaviour itself or behaviours that compete with or
support it[22]. These high-level constructs can be understood further using the Theoretical Domains Framework
(TDF)[25]. The more accurate this analysis of the target behaviour, the more likely it is that the intervention will
change the behaviour in the desired direction[22].
Aim: to identify what needs to change in the person and/or the environment in order to achieve the

secdrp__001_r.text:
desired
change in behaviour(s) as specified at the end of Stage 2a.
Study design: we will use five focus groups. The co-researchers agreed that focus groups are a good way to
answer our research questions. It was highlighted that the focus groups should be small, e.g. 3-5 people. Both
online and in-person options will be provided to conduct the focus groups. We will build on recruitment strategies
as established in previous Stages. Focus groups questions will be informed by both COM-B model[22] and
TDF[25]. We will also ask what could help participants overcome challenges/barriers in changing their behaviour
mentioned during the focus groups. In addition, we will also collect background information (e.g. EDI
characteristics) and ask about caring situation.
Participants and settings: unpaid carers of people living with dementia across England. Both former (within five
years[26]) and current carers will be eligible to take part, if they provided or provided unpaid support to a person
with dementia. They will have to be at least 18 years old. We will use similar recruitment strategies as in the
previous Stage. We will also approach participants who took part in the previous Stage if

secdrp__001_s.text:
they agreed to be
contacted.
Analysis: two researchers will independently code the transcripts. The COM-B model and TDF will be used as a
combined deductive framework for the analysis. The interview data will be managed using NVivo. SPSS will be
used for descriptive data analysis of participants' characteristics.
Outcome: understanding what influences behaviour through a behavioural diagnosis using the TDF and COM-B.
Stages 2c) Identifying intervention options; 25-30 months: research group discussions
Aim: to identify relevant intervention functions and policy categories following the COM-B and TDF analyses.
As a first step we will present findings from the COM-B and TDF analyses (Stage 2b) at SC meeting. We will
discuss which themes should be prioritised, e.g. using the criteria of frequency (e.g. how many times does
psychological capability feature as an influence in data we collected); elaboration (number of themes and sub-
themes within each domain) and expressed importance (e.g. quotes from study participants expressing
importance). Once we agree on key barriers and facilitators, these will be mapped against nine intervention
options and seven policy categories as outlined in

secdrp__001_t.text:
Behaviour Change Wheel[22]. The identified intervention
options and policy categories will be assessed with co-researchers and with an SC against APEASE criteria[22,
32]: acceptability, practicality, effectiveness, affordability, side-effects/safety and equity.
Stage 2d) Identifying content and implementation options; 25-29 months: research group discussions
Aim: to identify which techniques will deliver the intervention options and how the intervention will be put into
practice.
Behaviour change techniques are the active ingredients within the intervention, designed to change the behaviour
(e.g. goal setting, behavioural substitution, feedback on behaviour etc.). The behaviour change techniques will be
selected using Behaviour Change Techniques Taxonomy App as well as existing literature on what behaviour
change techniques are best for changing exercise/diet. The identified behaviour change techniques will be
assessed with co-researchers and with an SC against APEASE criteria[28, 32]. We will also identify behaviour
change techniques mentioned within the focus groups in Stage 2b. The discussion with co-researchers, SC and
the findings from focus groups will then inform the develop

secdrp__001_u.text:
ment of intervention. We will also discuss the most
appropriate style and mode of delivery of each technique (e.g., professional conversation, peer support buddies,
leaflets, website, mobile apps etc.)[22]. Decisions will also be made around who should deliver the intervention,
and the intensity, frequency and duration of the intervention.
Stage 2e) Gathering feedback on intervention; 28-33 months: focus groups
We will share the intervention with a range of stakeholders living across England. The final decision who should be
involved in these groups will depend on how the intervention will look and who may be delivering it. With co-
researchers, we have currently identified the following stakeholder groups: unpaid carers; other family members
and friends; health care professionals; people working in adult social care and social work. We will conduct up to
five focus groups with 3-5 people per group. Both online and in-person options will be provided to conduct the
focus groups. We will build on previous recruitment strategies.
Analysis: focus groups will be audio-recorded using encrypted digital voice recorders and transcribed. The
content of the focus groups will be analysed using

secdrp__001_v.text:
content analysis. The intervention will be adapted as needed.
Outcome of Stages 2c, 2d and 2e: the intervention will be developed.
Stage 3: Feasibility study; 30-44 months
Aim: to test whether the proposed intervention is feasible and acceptable for unpaid carers and those who may be
delivering it.
Study design: feasibility study conducted in England. A quantitative (e.g. surveys) and qualitative (e.g. semi-
structured interviews) mixed-methods design will be used to assess predefined progression criteria that relate to
the evaluation design (e.g. reducing uncertainty around recruitment, data collection, retention, outcomes, and
analysis) and the intervention itself (e.g. around optimal content and delivery, acceptability, adherence, or capacity
of providers to deliver the intervention).
Dissemination, Outputs and anticipated Impact
We will work closely with SC and co-researchers to develop and deliver a dissemination strategy that is impactful
and appropriate for different target audiences. Results from each Stage/study will be shared with participants and
participating health care and social care organisations. We will also disseminate results through SC and co-
researchers will

secdrp__001_w.text:
use their established links, e.g. Mobilise, Facebook groups, their own carers’ groups, Dementia
Friendly East Lothian, Local Advocacy groups etc. Co-researchers suggested the following outputs to disseminate
the findings: blog; leaflets; infographic; webinar; an article for Alzheimer’s Society journal or the UK Dementia
Journal. We will also write peer-reviewed papers and co-present the results at conferences.
Importantly after each Stage we will revisit and adapt our dissemination strategy. We will also adapt the
dissemination strategy based on the training obtained through this Fellowship.
I will also set up a project website/page aimed at academics and media outlets. We will also produce a 12-minute
UK based documentary with KMTV (TV made for Kent). The primary aim of this output is to disseminate the
research findings and to engage policy makers and media stakeholders. It will be transmitted on the Local
Television Network Channel 7 through KMTV, together with the coordination of associated press and publicity
campaigns undertaken with partner organisations.
We anticipate the following impact:
 By the end of this Fellowship a feasible and acceptable intervention targeting phy

secdrp__001_x.text:
sical activity, diet or time
management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia will be developed. Following this, the plan will
be to apply for funding to evaluate whether the proposed intervention works.
 Increased awareness of health and wellbeing concerns experienced by unpaid carers of people living with
dementia among health and social care professionals, policy makers and wider public.
 Other target behaviours for possible future interventions will be identified.
 This Fellowship addresses health and wellbeing concerns as identified by unpaid carers. This Fellowship
has a potential to improve how the unpaid carers are supported in terms of their own health and wellbeing
concerns by identifying who needs to be involved in supporting them, as well as any barriers and
facilitators to behaviour change.
 This Fellowship has a strong focus on EDI. Therefore, we will be able to share findings with range of
unpaid carers considering their gender, sexual orientation, ethnic background, socioeconomic status etc.
Project Management
I will be responsible for project co-ordination and management, budget management, ensuring effective
communications within the team, overseeing

secdrp__001_y.text:
our publication and dissemination strategy.
Within this Fellowship application I have requested funding for support staff (research associate) to the equivalent
of 50% FTE for a period of 44 months. We will also identify together any training needs (£3000 was requested to
support them). They will be part of project management group and attend SC meetings.
Project Management Group (PMG) will meet monthly to ensure the project is on track and discuss project related
issues. The group will include myself, support staff and eight co-researchers. Each member of the PMG will have
an opportunity to chair these meetings. In addition to attending monthly meetings, co-researchers expressed
interest in conducting the research itself including conducting focus groups, reviewing all study documents and
supporting dissemination activities. We agreed with co-researchers that on top of monthly meetings they may
contribute up to 10 hours per month to the research delivery. The type of activities and amount of commitment will
be decided with each co-researcher depending on their availability at that time as their situation and health can
fluctuate. Appropriate training will be provided and was incl

secdrp__001_z.text:
uded in the budget.
The SC will meet online at key stages of the project. The support staff, co-researchers and mentors will also
attend all SC meetings. SC will include academics with relevant expertise, health and social care professionals,
representatives from independent charities and commissioning officers.
The Brighton and Sussex Clinical Trials Unit (CTU): will support me in three areas: data management, study
management and statistical advice.
Ethics: before the start of the study, a favourable opinion will be sought from the Health Research Authority and/or
University of Kent. When needed Research Governance approvals will be sought from participating local
authorities and ADASS (Directors of Adult Social Care).
Success Criteria
The Fellowship will be a success if I develop feasible and acceptable intervention targeting physical activity and
diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia. The ongoing success criteria would be to
deliver each stage on time, recruit as intended and deliver outputs as planned.
Barriers/risks include the following:
 Failure to start on time due to delays in contract, ethical approval or recruitment. Risk low, allowing

secdrp__001_{.text:
enough
time between outcome of the competition and start of the Fellowship and allowing enough time for
securing approvals.
 Failure to recruit members to SC: Risk low, most have already agreed.
 Failure to recruit: Risk low, I have established relationships in the field and the focus of this Fellowship is
important as seen by unpaid carers, health care professionals and people working in adult social care.
Importantly I have previous experience with recruiting unpaid carers of people with dementia through
health care and social care. I will also draw on links of eight co-researchers. I will also be supported
through CRN.
 Failure to deliver each element on time. Risk: medium. I have allowed 3.5 years to deliver this Fellowship.
I also requested a support from research associate. Importantly I will be also supported by eight co-
researchers (e.g. 10 hours per month each to support e.g. recruitment, focus groups etc.), CTU, mentors
and by University of Kent.

secppi__001_a.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
The focus of this Fellowship is on unpaid carers of someone living with dementia. Therefore, unpaid carers played
a central role in co-producing this Fellowship application. Over the period of twelve months (January 2023-January
2024) we met together online on a monthly basis for two hours. Throughout these meetings we got to know each
other, we agreed how we wanted to work together, identified health and wellbeing concerns for unpaid carers,
what the wellbeing and health-related goals carers would like to work towards etc. We also spoke about any
barriers and facilitators when it comes to supporting carers to meet their own health and wellbeing goals. For
example, it was highlighted that: ‘It is important that professionals give advice that is purposeful and meaningful
and would allow carers to act on it. Often advice around lifestyle changes is not applicable to carers.’ In addition,
unpaid carers expressed that: ‘There is no size fits all, the intervention has to be tailored to individual
circumstances, it is important to speak to people and to listen to them.’ In our

secppi__001_b.text:
meetings, we also spoke together
about each stage. We specifically focused on what methods are the most appropriate way to collect the
information, who should be the target population, how we would like to disseminate the results etc. They also
provided feedback on the application itself to ensure that their voices run as a golden thread through this
application.
We were a team of ten people. Eight people self-identified as a former or current unpaid carer living across
England and Scotland. Seven co-researchers will continue to work on the Fellowship as co-researchers – this
means they will be part of the core research team; they will own the research and be responsible for it. As one
person passed away we will recruit a new co-researcher for this Fellowship. The co-researchers themselves
represent a range of perspectives in terms of their experiences as carers. Importantly the group also included
individuals with diverse characteristics: e.g. having English as a second language; being black, belonging to
LGBTQ+ community and coming from range of socioeconomic backgrounds.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in

secppi__001_c.text:
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
Seven unpaid carers will continue to work on the Fellowship as co-researchers. As one person passed away we
will recruit a new co-researcher for this Fellowship. We will specifically recruit someone from a minority
background to further diversify the voices represented in our group.
The co-researchers will participate in monthly two hour online project management meetings. The frequency,
format and length of meetings were suggested by co-researchers themselves and this worked well for us in the
last twelve months. If the person cannot attend the meeting, they will be offered a replacement meeting – a
practice that we established while working together on this application. Each person will receive ahead of the
meeting study packages (either printed or electronic version) including any materials that will be discussed during
the meeting. Co-researchers will have an opportunity to chair these meetings and facilitate any breakout groups
during the meetings. They will be paid for their attendance at the meetings including contribution to their internet
costs. Importantly to allow them to contribute we also budgeted for

secppi__001_d.text:
carer replacement costs: some carers of
people living with dementia may require having someone in place instead of them to look after a person living with
dementia while working on the project. Co-researchers will also attend all SC meetings.
In addition to attending monthly meetings, co-researchers expressed interest in conducting the research itself
including conducting focus groups, reviewing all study documents, and supporting dissemination activities. We
agreed with co-researchers that on top of monthly meetings they may contribute up to 10 hours per month to the
research delivery. The type of activities and amount of commitment will be decided with each co-researcher
depending on their availability at that time as their situation and health can fluctuate.
Co-researchers will be provided with training to supplement and enhance their existing skills so they are able to
carry out research activities related to the project. Each of them will also be able to attend two conferences
throughout the Fellowship.
If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/service users, carers and the
public in your proposed research, please justify why
Not applicabl

sectdars__001_a.text:
Proposed training and development programme
1. Training focused on behaviour change interventions: The proposed research is informed by
Behaviour Change Wheel. In 2022-2023 I attended two courses in Behaviour Change Wheel: Behaviour
Change Interventions: Introductory Principles and Practice (introductory) and Applied Principles and
Practice of Behaviour Change (intermediate) to support the development of this application. During the
Fellowship I would like to gain more in depth-knowledge of Behaviour Change Wheel and therefore I
propose to attend two Advanced Modules in Behaviour Change: Principles and Practice of Behavioural
Systems Mapping and the Behaviour Change Intervention Ontology (BCIO): How to use the BCIO for
different research and applied purposes. I plan to attend these courses during the first year of the
Fellowship. These courses are delivered by the developers of the model based at Centre for Behaviour
Change, UCL. In addition to gaining a specific knowledge, attending these courses will also allow me to
connect to other researchers interested in this specific approach. Importantly, Behaviour Change Wheel
represents one of the approaches to behaviour change. There ar
sectdars__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_b.text:
e other approaches to developing
interventions e.g. Intervention Mapping which provides a framework for developing theory-based and
evidence-based interventions applied to health promotion and disease prevention. To be able, in the
future, to support other researchers aiming to improve the wellbeing and health of unpaid carers of people
with dementia, I also propose to attend a 5-day summer course on Intervention Mapping: Designing
Theory-based and Evidence-based Programs at Maastricht University in the Netherlands. Being trained in
two key approaches to behaviour change intervention development will enable me to work beyond this
Fellowship on a range of projects and connect with people who apply these approaches in their work. I
plan to attend this course in the second year of the Fellowship.
2. Training in Co-production: as a researcher I am committed to work in a co-produced way together with
unpaid carers of people living with dementia, other academics, health and social care professionals. While
co-producing this application over the period of twelve months together with unpaid carers I have identified
two key areas where I need additional training: 1. working with conflict an
sectdars__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_c.text:
d confrontation in teams; and 2.
facilitation skills. As such I plan to attend three courses delivered by Gestalt Centre in London during the
first year of Fellowship: Group Facilitation: Key concepts, skills and personal development (one day in
person workshop); Working with Conflict and Confrontation in Groups and Teams (two days in person
workshop); and Group Facilitation Training Intensive (nine days in total spread over four months).
3. Equality, Diversity and Inclusion (EDI): As a researcher, I am aware that the research I conduct mainly
reflects experiences of those who self-identify as e.g. white, British, heterosexual and from middle class.
As such in this Fellowship, I intend to ensure that we will take proactive steps to recruit people from
diverse backgrounds – this includes co-researchers, members of SC and research participants. While as
an EDI representative at University of Kent I have opportunities to attend a range of training sessions, in
this Fellowship I would like to specifically attend training sessions related to EDI issues when conducting
research. Therefore, I plan to attend following training throughout the Fellowship: a) Improving the
inclusion of under-
sectdars__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_d.text:
served groups in health research (online webinar by INVOLVE); and b) An Ethical
Perspective on Implementing Equal Opportunities in PPI (online webinar). Importantly as part of the NIHR
Applied Research Collaboration I am member of a working group of researchers interested in the inclusion
of individuals living with dementia and their carers in research.
4. Impact and dissemination training: I aim to become a researcher who has an influence beyond
academia. Specifically, I would like to make the needs of unpaid carers visible to present and future health
and social care professionals as well as policy makers. I plan to attend the following courses: a) Impact
Training for Researchers (online course provided by Fast Track Impact over 5 weeks; first year of the
Fellowship) and Compelling Communication Skills (6 week course delivered by University of Cambridge;
second year of the Fellowship). In addition, I will apply for the Royal Society Pairing Scheme. Each year
30 research scientists are paired with UK parliamentarians and civil servants. They learn about each
other’s work by spending time together in Westminster and the researcher’s institutions. Those taking part
gain an insight i
sectdars__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_e.text:
nto how research findings can help inform policy making and come away with a better
understanding of how they can get involved.
5. Leadership training: I would describe a good leader in three words: kind; adaptive and self-aware. I had
an opportunity to attend the NIHR Virtual Leadership Programme for social care researchers which was a
scaled down version of NIHR Future-Focused Leadership Programme. As the content of that course
reflected my own values, I would like to attend the NIHR Future-Focused Leadership Programme as part
of this Fellowship.
6. Software skills: I would like to become an expert on two software packages I regularly use in my
research and will be also central to delivery of this Fellowship: NVivo and Qualtrics. As such I suggest
attending two courses: NVivo Expert Certification: this will enable me to be an in-house NVivo expert and
advisor for other researchers across University of Kent. I will also do Qualtrics online courses, again
allowing me to raise my profile in the research community.
7. Specific skills and knowledge needed for the next stage of the project (after Fellowship):
 Implementation Science Training: designed to build capacity and prepare for
sectdars__001_e.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_f.text:
implementation in particular
settings; third year of the Fellowship.
 Economic Evaluation Online: An introductory course; third year of the Fellowship.
8. Conferences: Conferences are an incredible opportunity to present one’s own research, to learn about up
to date evidence and make important connections. As such I plan to attend at least one conference
between years 2-3 of the Fellowship. I will focus on conferences in three areas: dementia; health
psychology/behaviour change; and ageing.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1
Name Dr Rosie Ashworth
Institution NHS Scotland Neuroprogressive and Dementia Network
Position The Lead for the Patient and Public Involvement group
Description of support provided
Rosie leads a group known as ‘Partners in Research’ that includes people living with dementia and their unpaid
carers. They work together on a range of activities including book writing, developing research proposals,
reviewing research materials and raising awareness. Rosie is also a Research Fellow at the University of
Edinburgh with a special interest in co-produced research.
Rosie played a central role in development of this Fellowship application as she provided me with advice on
sectdars__001_f.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_g.text:
co-
production in the last twelve months. She also helped me to co-facilitate the majority of the meetings with unpaid
carers in the last twelve months as we worked together on this application. We have worked together on co-
producing a blog, academic paper, podcast episode and another funding application.
Rosie will continue to support me regularly throughout the Fellowship. She will provide advice on any issues with
co-production, co-facilitate meetings with unpaid carers when needed and she will be part of SC. We will have
monthly online meetings to discuss the progress of this Fellowship. She will also be involved in key research
outputs from this Fellowship.
Importantly, Rosie has established links with other researchers in dementia working in a co-produced way and will
help me to network with other academics working in similar area. We plan to establish a peer support group for
researchers in dementia who use co-production. In addition, we plan to co-produce, together with unpaid carers of
people living with dementia, a training course on co-production for researchers working in dementia.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 2
Name Dr Sarah Hotham
Institution Centre for Health Ser
sectdars__001_g.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_h.text:
vices Studies (CHSS), University of Kent
Position Senior Research Fellow
Description of support provided
Sarah is a Senior Research Fellow and BPS Chartered Psychologist. She is an expert in behavioural health
psychology, with a specific focus on areas of nutrition (breastfeeding, obesity and malnutrition), sanitation and
training of healthcare professionals. Her current and previous research has focused on three UN Sustainable
Development Goals: Zero hunger; Good health and wellbeing (i.e. to decrease the risk of non-communicable
diseases); and Clean water and sanitation.
Sarah has an extensive expertise in supervising projects with a focus on behavioural science approaches to
design, delivery and evaluation of health behaviour change interventions. She is also interested in Implementation
Science approaches to evaluation and how evidence can be translated into policy and practice.
Sarah will provide me with expertise during the Fellowship on the design of the health behaviour change
intervention as well as the feasibility study. Importantly, Implementation Science is an area where I lack an
experience and this is the area where I would like to gain more knowledge. Sarah will supp
sectdars__001_h.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_i.text:
ort me with this. We will
meet regularly once per two months to discuss the progress of the Fellowship. She will also be involved in the
outputs, and she will be member of SC.
Importantly, Sarah will also support me to think about my career beyond this Fellowship. She is based at Kent and
will mentor me in advancing my own career here and beyond.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 3
Name Professor Naji Tabet
Institution Brighton & Sussex Medical School
Position Professor, Dementia and Old Age Psychiatry
Description of support provided
Naji is the Director of the Centre for Dementia Studies (CDS) and the Course Leader of the MSc Dementia Studies
at Brighton and Sussex Medical School. He also leads the Dementia Theme at the NIHR Applied Research
Collaboration Kent, Surrey and Sussex. He is also the Dementia Speciality Co-Lead for NIHR Clinical Research
Network Kent, Surrey and Sussex.
Naji has led the unit on several important phase II-IV clinical trials trying to advance the treatment of Alzheimer’s
disease. Importantly he is also involved in studies that investigate the role of non-pharmacological interventions,
lifestyles and co-morbid physical illnesses in disease progression. He has
sectdars__001_i.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_j.text:
been investigating the role of
antioxidants, dietary and endogenous, and mechanistic metabolites in Alzheimer’s disease and their interaction
with currently available treatments. In addition to his research activities, clinically, he holds an Honorary Consultant
post at Sussex Partnership NHS Foundation Trust where he delivers a diagnostic and treatment Memory
Assessment Service to Trust patients with memory complaints.
Naji will support me throughout the Fellowship and we will meet once per three months to discuss the progress of
the Fellowship. He will also help me to think about next steps beyond the Fellowship. He will be involved in the
outputs and be a member of SC.
Naji is a practicing clinician and therefore I will use our conversations to better understand how people with
dementia and their unpaid carers are supported in the NHS.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 4
Name Professor Lindsay Forbes
Institution Centre for Health Services Studies (CHSS), University of Kent
Position Clinical Professor of Public Health
Description of support provided
Lindsay is Clinical Professor of Public Health at the Centre for Health Services Studies (CHSS) at the University of
Kent and Theme Lead
sectdars__001_j.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_k.text:
for Public Health at the NIHR Applied Research Collaboration Kent, Surrey and Sussex.
Throughout her public health career, Lindsay has worked across academia and public health policy and
implementation and has extensive experience of developing and evaluating complex interventions. She has well-
established links with local government, running a research network across six local authorities and leading
academic input into NIHR Health Determinants Research Collaboration with Medway Council. This means that she
will be able to facilitate links with local authorities, support study design, execution and analysis, and advice on
dissemination strategies, particularly focusing on policymakers. In particular, Lindsay will help me focus on the
next steps – how the effectiveness of the intervention can be evaluated; how it can be best implemented in the real
world and how its impact on health and wellbeing can be measured. She will be part of SC.
Collaborations
I plan to undertake the award at Centre for Health Services Studies (CHSS). CHSS is a centre of research
excellence which undertakes high quality research into a wide range of health and social care systems and health
and care servi
sectdars__001_k.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_l.text:
ces issues at local, national and international levels. CHSS builds the evidence base for policy and
practice to prevent ill-health, promote wellbeing and meet needs for care, focusing on primary and community
services, public health and social care working with academic collaborators across the UK and the world. CHSS
works with local government and health services across Kent, Surrey and Sussex to build research capacity and
culture and address the key health and social care research needs across the region.
CHSS has a substantial number of links with other organisations including National Institute of Health Research
(NIHR) Applied Research Collaboration Kent, Surrey and Sussex (ARC KSS) and the Kent Adult Social Care
Partnership. ARC KSS is the national lead for social care. Primary and Community Care and Living Well with
Dementia are also key themes of the ARC, with active and interlinked research programmes. There are well-
established relationships between the ARC theme leads (primary care lead is Professor Harm van Marwijk in
Brighton and Sussex Medical School and Professor Naji Tabet (my mentor) is theme lead for dementia). As an
employee of University of Kent I have an acc
sectdars__001_l.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_m.text:
ess to senior researchers who are part of ARC and introductory
emails will be sent by Professor Lindsay Forbes (my mentor) and/or Professor Naji Tabet. In addition, I will draw
on a network of Professor Lindsay Forbes’s local contacts in public health and primary care. Furthermore, I will join
the Society of Academic Primary Care (SAPC) which would connect me to all the country's leading researchers in
primary care.
Kent Adult Social Care Partnership led by Professor Ann-Marie Towers (University of Kent) supports the social
care sector in Kent by conducting high quality research relating to local social care priorities. One of the key
elements of the Kent Adult Social Care Partnership are Communities of Practice which bring together people who
have different expertise but a shared goal. Professor Towers is my line manager and she will support me in
accessing links across adult social care.
Importantly being based at Kent is also strategically important as Kent, Surrey and Sussex are in an ideal position
to lead on ageing and living well with dementia research. As a region, we have a higher proportion of older people,
and people living with dementia and our ageing population is grow
sectdars__001_m.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_n.text:
ing faster than the national average. While
working at University of Kent, I have formed relationships with NIHR Clinical Research Network Kent, Surrey and
Sussex and independent charities such as Age UK Canterbury, Carers Support West Sussex, Alzheimer’s Society
(local branches) and commissioning officers based locally. I would continue to work closely with them during the
award and seek their expertise and advice.
Lastly, I will be supported by the Brighton and Sussex Clinical Trials Unit (CTU) that provides support and
expertise for clinical researchers in the South East in the development of funding applications, trial design, study
management, quality assurance, data analysis, dissemination and trial close-down. We identified together that I
will require support/mentorship in three areas: data management, study management and statistical advice.
Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support must be completed by the head of your lead academic department. In order to
contribute to this application, this individual must be invited to do so via the 'Participants and Signatories' section of
the form.
Statement of support
12 January 2024
As Director of the C
sectdars__001_n.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_o.text:
entre for Health Services Studies (CHSS), within the Division of Law, Society and Social
Justice at the University of Kent, I am delighted to support Dr Barbora Silarova’s application for an NIHR
Advanced Fellowshipand confirm that we will ensure she is provided with all necessary support and be able to
draw on the wider services of the University in order to deliver the work in time and within budget. CHSS is a
dynamic research unit delivering high quality, generalisable, applied health and care research that addresses
specific health or care issues locally, regionally, nationally and internationally.
We provide a welcoming and supportive environment for Early and Mid-Career Researchers and are committed to
support her employment at CHSS. We have a strong track record of supporting the development of Early and Mid-
Career Researchers, many of whom have progressed their careers at CHSS and are now leading their own
research projects as independent researchers. CHSS is well placed to support her proposed research through the
NIHR Applied Research Collaboration Kent, Surrey and Sussex (ARC KSS) and the Kent Research Partnership,
which is one of six NIHR funded social care research ca
sectdars__001_o.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_p.text:
pacity partnerships in England.
Dr Silarova will be a full member of our staff team, currently 60 researchers, and will have opportunities to engage
in all the activities run by the Centre and the University for Research Staff. She will have dedicated office space
and equipment for completing her research. Two of her mentors: Professor Lindsay Forbes and Dr Sarah Hotham
are based at CHSS. They will support Dr Silarova with the delivery of the Fellowship itself but also with her career
beyond the Fellowship and help her to identify leadership opportunities across CHSS and at University of Kent.
Dr Silarova will benefit from being based in an active research environment, with a strong track record in health
and social care, including several completed and ongoing NIHR grants relating to the needs of and outcomes of
informal carers. Dr Silarova will also benefit from strong links with ARC KSS, the Personal Social Services
Research Unit (PSSRU) and the NIHR School for Social Care (of which Kent is a founding member). Through the
ARC KSS, she will have access to implementation, co-production and Public and Patient Involvement and
Engagement support and the researcher development program
sectdars__001_p.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_q.text:
mes of the ARC KSS Academy (led by Professor
Kendall and Dr Julie MacInnes, also in CHSS).
Institutionally, we have an established track record of developing the careers of Early and Mid-Career Researchers
to a level where they have national and international recognition as experts in the field of health and social care.
The University of Kent is a signatory of the Researcher Development Concordat and supports its principles as part
of our commitment to create the very best culture for our researchers. Within CHSS we also have a researcher
development programme and regularly run training sessions, organise writing days and engage in a wider
programme of seminars and career development activities. CHSS has a strong track record in researcher
development with a clear development pathway for Early and Mid-Career Researchers.
We have been at the forefront of driving EDI activity across the organisation, establishing an Executive Lead for
EDI bringing together different streams to make sure EDI is underpinned in research innovations systems and
organisationally embedding an inclusive research culture. The University is building on its achievement of an
Athena Swan Bronze to apply for a
sectdars__001_q.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_r.text:
Silver Award, applying for AdvanceHE’s Race Equality Charter and becoming
a Disability Confident Leader organisation. EDI falls under the remit of the Deputy Director of Division, who
ensures EDI performance is collaboratively operationalised with staff providing EDI training for selection panels,
with physical and virtual environments following principles of inclusive design, and the availability of reasonable
adjustments for all staff needing access to workable and sustainable arrangements.
This Fellowship will provide Dr Silarova with an opportunity to consolidate her research and develop new research
programmes. She will be a member of both subject and disciplinary groups within CHSS, providing links with
complementary research projects, research methods support, and external partners in the health and care
community. She will have a senior researcher as a line manager (Professor Ann-Marie Towers) in addition to her
mentors. Dr Silarova is a third supervisor for one of our PhD students (start of PhD September 2023; focus on
unpaid carers of people living with dementia). We will identify together further opportunities for PhD supervision
throughout her Fellowship.
Health and soc
sectdars__001_r.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_s.text:
ial care research are strategic priorities for the University with new investments including the Kent
and Medway Medical School and an Institute for Health and Social Care launched this year. CHSS also has well
established links with the health and social care community and embedded roles in NIHR through the NIHR
Research Design Service South East, ARC KSS and the NIHR research capacity building Kent Research
Partnership. CHSS is also working collaboratively with the NIHR Clinical Research Network and has joint research
support arrangements with Kent and Medway Integrated Care Board and several NHS trusts, local authorities and
third sector organisations including, for example, Kent Adult Social Care team at Kent County Council. This
provides an extensive community relevant to her proposed research. In addition, Dr Silarova will be part of the
wider social science community as part of the University’s Division of Law, Society and Social Justice with access
to the Division’s academic programmes and development opportunities.
This project has real potential to create impact in the short, medium and long-term and as director of the centre I
fully support this application. Through coll
sectdars__001_s.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_t.text:
aborations, research capacity building and working with the wider Kent
community this application will produce high impact research. The work is likely to also enable future funding for Dr
Silarova to establish herself as an independent research leader. I unreservedly support this application.
sectdars__001_t.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

Item Description: PI conference attendance years 2 and 3
Justification of Cost: Conferences are an incredible opportunity to present one’s own research, to learn about up
to date evidence and make important connections. As such I plan to attend at least one conference between years
2-3 of the Fellowship. As such I will focus on conferences in three areas: dementia; health psychology/behaviour
change; and ageing. Following conferences will be considered: 1. Dementia: Alzheimer Europe Conference; UK
Dementia Congress; Alzheimer's Society Annual Conference; International Conference of Alzheimer’s Disease
International; Alzheimer’s Association International Conference; Annual Liverpool Dementia & Ageing Research
Conference; 2. Health psychology/behaviour change: UK Society for Behavioural Medicine Annual Scientific
Meeting; the Annual Conference of the European Health Psychology Society; 3. Ageing: Annual Conference of the
British Society of Gerontology

Item Description: Training and development: support staff
Justification of Cost: Within this Fellowship application I have requested funding for support staff. We will also

secsb__001_t.text:

857 857 858 428 3000
Type of cost: HEI
Description: Short Courses
Item Description: Training focused on Behaviour Change Wheel
Justification of Cost: The proposed research is informed by Behaviour Change Wheel. During the Fellowship I
would like to gain more in depth-knowledge of Behaviour Change Wheel and therefore I propose to attend two
Advanced Modules in Behaviour Change: Principles and Practice of Behavioural Systems Mapping (£400) and the
Behaviour Change Intervention Ontology (BCIO): How to use the BCIO for different research and applied
purposes (£400). I plan to attend these courses during the first year of the Fellowship. These courses are
delivered by the developers of the model based at Centre for Behaviour Change, UCL. In addition to gaining a
specific knowledge, attending these courses will also allow me to connect to other researchers interested in this
specific approach.
Year 1 Year 2 Year 3 Year 4 Total Cost (£)
800 0 0 0 800
Type of cost: HEI
Description: Short Courses
Item Description: Training focused on behaviour change interventions: Inter

secsb__001_u.text:
vention mapping
Justification of Cost: Behaviour Change Wheel represents one of the approaches to behaviour change. There
are other approaches to developing interventions e.g. Intervention Mapping which provides a framework for
developing theory-based and evidence-based interventions applied to health promotion and disease prevention.
To be able, in the future, to support other researchers aiming to improve the wellbeing and health of unpaid carers
of people with dementia, I also propose to attend a 5-day summer course on Intervention Mapping: Designing
Theory-based and Evidence-based Programs at Maastricht University in the Netherlands. Being trained in two key
approaches to behaviour change intervention development will enable me to work beyond this Fellowship on a
range of projects and connect with people who apply these approaches in their work. I plan to attend this course in
the second year of the Fellowship.

Item Description: Training in Co-production
Justification of Cost: As a researcher I am committed to work in a co-produced way together with unpaid carers
of people li

secsb__001_v.text:
ving with dementia, other academics, health and social care professionals. While co-producing this
application over the period of twelve months together with unpaid carers I have identified two key areas where I
need additional training: 1. working with conflict and confrontation in teams; and 2. facilitation skills. As such I plan
to attend three courses delivered by Gestalt Centre in London during the first year of Fellowship: Group
Facilitation: Key concepts, skills and personal development (one day in person workshop); Working with Conflict
and Confrontation in Groups and Teams (two days in person workshop); and Group Facilitation Training Intensive
(nine days in total spread over four months).

sub_criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
sub_criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision focused on unpaid carers' wellbeing and a distinct career trajectory towards leadership (g.1.a, g.1.b). The training plan is exceptionally detailed, covering specific skill gaps like conflict resolution and Behaviour Change Wheel, directly linked to project needs (g.1.c). Strong mentorship is evidenced by four supervisors with complementary expertise and structured meeting schedules (g.1.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive ideas' could be further emphasized against the broader landscape of dementia research, though the co-production element provides strong differentiation.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive ideas' could be further emphasized against the broader landscape of dementia research, though the co-production element provides strong differentiation.
