Professional
Not a Registered Health or Care Professional
Background
Profession (Not a Registered Health or Care Professional)
Psychologist
Please describe your research career to date
I am a psychologist who works in adult social care research with particular interest in family members and friends
who support someone living with dementia (unpaid carers). There are two key messages from unpaid carers that
inspired this Fellowship: ‘carers are never asked what they needed/what they want’ and ‘you can’t pour from an
empty jug.’ As such, I see this NIHR Advanced Fellowship as a beginning of my long-term commitment to help
unpaid carers to have good overall health and wellbeing.
I have an MSc (research) in Clinical and Health Psychology, MSc in Psychology and PhD in Medical Sciences. I
have ten years’ experience in coordinating and successfully executing various research projects. I have acquired
experience in all stages of the research process – from protocol development, ethics, conduct of studies of
different designs (both quantitative and qualitative) through to dissemination of the results. Since 2014, I have built
a research profile in Health, Social Care and Wellbeing. To date,

he year, we continued to meet monthly to
discuss all work packages proposed in this Fellowship, dissemination activities and their role in the Fellowship if
awarded. Alongside this Fellowship application, we also co-produced a blog[1] and we engaged with an illustrator
who captured our work on how unpaid carers of people living with dementia can address some of their concerns
when it comes to their own health and wellbeing. In addition, our team also applied together with a local artist for
SHAPE Involve and Engage grant from the British Academy (£8000, submitted in June 2023, unsuccessful) to
enable us to hold a mixed-media workshop with unpaid carers of people living with dementia followed by an
exhibition to increase awareness of what it means to be a carer for a person with dementia. We are also working
together on academic paper and a podcast episode (for Dementia Researcher) to reflect on our experience of co-
producing this Fellowship application.
To conclude, this NIHR Advanced Fellowship represents an incredible opportunity for a step change in my career
trajectory – a crucial step of establishing myself as a future leader in dementia research. Namely, over the course
of t

he Fellowship I would like to start to generate knowledge on how we can support unpaid carers of people living
with dementia to look after their own health and wellbeing. Importantly, I would like for this knowledge to be co-
produced together with unpaid carers. This will ensure that the research I will be leading on will be meaningful to
unpaid carers, driven by their own needs. Ultimately as a researcher, I would like to make the needs of unpaid
carers visible to academic community, present and future health and social care professionals as well as policy
makers.
Title of research degree PhD
Has the degree already
Yes
been awarded?
Date of Award 27/01/2014
Has this application, or a similar application, previously been submitted to this or any other funding body?
No
Contextual Factors
Please use this question to detail any contextual factors you wish to make the Selection Committee aware of.
NIHR wants to know about any circumstances so that they may take them into consideration during the
assessment of your application. Contextual factors may include:
 Career breaks due to parental leave, or periods of illness.
 Reduced time spent undertaking research due to a disability or c

Proposed training and development programme
1. Training focused on behaviour change interventions: The proposed research is informed by
Behaviour Change Wheel. In 2022-2023 I attended two courses in Behaviour Change Wheel: Behaviour
Change Interventions: Introductory Principles and Practice (introductory) and Applied Principles and
Practice of Behaviour Change (intermediate) to support the development of this application. During the
Fellowship I would like to gain more in depth-knowledge of Behaviour Change Wheel and therefore I
propose to attend two Advanced Modules in Behaviour Change: Principles and Practice of Behavioural
Systems Mapping and the Behaviour Change Intervention Ontology (BCIO): How to use the BCIO for
different research and applied purposes. I plan to attend these courses during the first year of the
Fellowship. These courses are delivered by the developers of the model based at Centre for Behaviour
Change, UCL. In addition to gaining a specific knowledge, attending these courses will also allow me to
connect to other researchers interested in this specific approach. Importantly, Behaviour Change Wheel
represents one of the approaches to behaviour change. There ar
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
e other approaches to developing
interventions e.g. Intervention Mapping which provides a framework for developing theory-based and
evidence-based interventions applied to health promotion and disease prevention. To be able, in the
future, to support other researchers aiming to improve the wellbeing and health of unpaid carers of people
with dementia, I also propose to attend a 5-day summer course on Intervention Mapping: Designing
Theory-based and Evidence-based Programs at Maastricht University in the Netherlands. Being trained in
two key approaches to behaviour change intervention development will enable me to work beyond this
Fellowship on a range of projects and connect with people who apply these approaches in their work. I
plan to attend this course in the second year of the Fellowship.
2. Training in Co-production: as a researcher I am committed to work in a co-produced way together with
unpaid carers of people living with dementia, other academics, health and social care professionals. While
co-producing this application over the period of twelve months together with unpaid carers I have identified
two key areas where I need additional training: 1. working with conflict an

sub_criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
sub_criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
sub_criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly states the Fellowship is a 'crucial step' to establish independence and leadership, clearly articulating why this award is the right next step (g.2.a, g.2.b). The proposal details how the award will build on existing experience by enabling new skills in Implementation Science and Economic Evaluation (g.2.c). Partnerships with NHS, social care, and third-sector organizations are well-developed (g.2.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The specific long-term impact of these new partnerships on the applicant's career beyond the Fellowship duration could be slightly more elaborated, though the current evidence is strong.
sub_criteria.rationale: The specific long-term impact of these new partnerships on the applicant's career beyond the Fellowship duration could be slightly more elaborated, though the current evidence is strong.

nto how research findings can help inform policy making and come away with a better
understanding of how they can get involved.
5. Leadership training: I would describe a good leader in three words: kind; adaptive and self-aware. I had
an opportunity to attend the NIHR Virtual Leadership Programme for social care researchers which was a
scaled down version of NIHR Future-Focused Leadership Programme. As the content of that course
reflected my own values, I would like to attend the NIHR Future-Focused Leadership Programme as part
of this Fellowship.
6. Software skills: I would like to become an expert on two software packages I regularly use in my
research and will be also central to delivery of this Fellowship: NVivo and Qualtrics. As such I suggest
attending two courses: NVivo Expert Certification: this will enable me to be an in-house NVivo expert and
advisor for other researchers across University of Kent. I will also do Qualtrics online courses, again
allowing me to raise my profile in the research community.
7. Specific skills and knowledge needed for the next stage of the project (after Fellowship):
 Implementation Science Training: designed to build capacity and prepare for

settings; third year of the Fellowship.
 Economic Evaluation Online: An introductory course; third year of the Fellowship.
8. Conferences: Conferences are an incredible opportunity to present one’s own research, to learn about up
to date evidence and make important connections. As such I plan to attend at least one conference
between years 2-3 of the Fellowship. I will focus on conferences in three areas: dementia; health
psychology/behaviour change; and ageing.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1
Name Dr Rosie Ashworth
Institution NHS Scotland Neuroprogressive and Dementia Network
Position The Lead for the Patient and Public Involvement group
Description of support provided
Rosie leads a group known as ‘Partners in Research’ that includes people living with dementia and their unpaid
carers. They work together on a range of activities including book writing, developing research proposals,
reviewing research materials and raising awareness. Rosie is also a Research Fellow at the University of
Edinburgh with a special interest in co-produced research.
Rosie played a central role in development of this Fellowship application as she provided me with advice on

ort me with this. We will
meet regularly once per two months to discuss the progress of the Fellowship. She will also be involved in the
outputs, and she will be member of SC.
Importantly, Sarah will also support me to think about my career beyond this Fellowship. She is based at Kent and
will mentor me in advancing my own career here and beyond.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 3
Name Professor Naji Tabet
Institution Brighton & Sussex Medical School
Position Professor, Dementia and Old Age Psychiatry
Description of support provided
Naji is the Director of the Centre for Dementia Studies (CDS) and the Course Leader of the MSc Dementia Studies
at Brighton and Sussex Medical School. He also leads the Dementia Theme at the NIHR Applied Research
Collaboration Kent, Surrey and Sussex. He is also the Dementia Speciality Co-Lead for NIHR Clinical Research
Network Kent, Surrey and Sussex.
Naji has led the unit on several important phase II-IV clinical trials trying to advance the treatment of Alzheimer’s
disease. Importantly he is also involved in studies that investigate the role of non-pharmacological interventions,
lifestyles and co-morbid physical illnesses in disease progression. He has

sub_criteria.name: Relevant Research Experience and Training
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant has 10 years of experience across all research stages, including RCTs, qualitative methods, and systematic reviews, directly relevant to the proposed study (g.3.a). Formal training is evidenced by previous grants (NIHR, ESRC) and specific certifications (g.3.b). There is a perfect alignment between prior experience in dementia and health services research and the skills required for this project (g.3.c).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for relevant experience and training is comprehensive and directly aligned with the proposal.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for relevant experience and training is comprehensive and directly aligned with the proposal.

signals.signal_text: Number and quality of publications, reports, or presentations
Citation metrics or other indicators of impact (where mentioned)
Awards, grants, or recognitions linked to prior research
Description of practical or societal impact of previous work

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant has a robust publication record (20+ papers, h-index 13) in peer-reviewed journals (g.4.a). Citation metrics and specific grants (NIHR, ESRC) indicate strong impact (g.4.b, g.4.c). The applicant describes practical impact through co-produced knowledge and engagement with policy makers (g.4.d).

I have co-authored 33 publications (12 as a first
author and 11 as a second author) in peer-reviewed academic journals. According to ResearchGate, I have been
cited 538 times, with an h-index 13. I worked as a researcher in the Netherlands, the USA and in England. I
presented the research at international conferences focusing on health psychology, public health and adult social
care. Importantly, I also ensure that outcomes of my research reach diverse audiences, including talks at carers’
organisations and blogs focusing on unpaid carers.
My early postdoctoral career focused on developing and evaluating behavioural change interventions. For
example, as a Career Development Fellow at University of Cambridge (2014-2016), I tried to understand the
impact of communicating risk estimates on physicians' and patients' behaviour, wellbeing, and other relevant
clinical outcomes. I used range of study designs including randomised controlled trial (RCT), qualitative interviews
and focus groups as well as systematic reviews. Specifically, I was leading INFORM trial (Chief Investigator:
Professor Simon Griffin), open labelled, parallel group RCT of cardiovascular disease risk communication. An

integral part of INFORM was a personalised web-based lifestyle programme to prevent cardiovascular disease
that I adapted for UK environment from American Heart to Health study. This has resulted in several papers with
me being a first author on two key papers: the paper reporting the results from the RCT and protocol paper.
I expanded what I learned from cardiovascular disease prevention on dementia prevention during my time as a
Research Fellow at University of Exeter (2017-2019). For example, as part of a wider project, I secured ESRC
Impact Acceleration Account Business Boost funding (approx. £10,000) for the MOVE study to allow us to initiate
collaboration with a mobile health application developer (based in Denmark) and to establish partnerships with
representatives of key stakeholders (e.g. patients, health care professionals, commissioners and policy makers).
During this time, I also became an Early Career Representative as part of International Research Network on
Dementia Prevention. This resulted in an editorial (in Aging & Mental Health) co-authored with five international
researchers highlighting that the career development of early- and mid-career researchers in deme

Evidence of increasing responsibility in previous roles (e.g., leading projects, mentoring)
Participation in leadership or professional development programs
Recognition by peers or institutions suggesting leadership potential

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant clearly states career ambitions to become an independent research leader (g.5.a). Evidence of increasing responsibility is shown through leading trials (INFORM), securing funding, and serving as an Early Career Representative (g.5.b). Participation in leadership programs (Springboard, GW4 Crucible) is explicitly mentioned (g.5.c). Recognition by peers is implied through grant success and representative roles (g.5.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Direct quotes or testimonials from peers regarding leadership potential are not explicitly included, though the track record strongly implies it.
sub_criteria.rationale: Direct quotes or testimonials from peers regarding leadership potential are not explicitly included, though the track record strongly implies it.

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant demonstrates a clear commitment to a practitioner-academic career through roles at NIHR Research Design Service and the Dementia Centre (g.6.a, g.6.b). Please describe your research career to date
I am a psychologist who works in adult social care research with particular interest in family members and friends
who support someone living with dementia (unpaid carers). There are two key messages from unpaid carers that
inspired this Fellowship: ‘carers are never asked what they needed/what they want’ and ‘you can’t pour from an
empty jug.’ As such, I see this NIHR Advanced Fellowship as a beginning of my long-term commitment to help
unpaid carers to have good overall health and wellbeing.
I have an MSc (research) in Clinical and Health Psychology, MSc in Psychology and PhD in Medical Sciences. I
have ten years’ experience in coordinating and successfully executing various research projects. I have acquired
experience in all stages of the research process – from protocol development, ethics, conduct of studies of
different designs (both quantitative and qualitative) through to dissemination of the results. Since 2014, I have built
a research profile in Health, Social Care and Wellbeing. To date,

ntia should be a
global priority.
I started to be interested in a topic of wellbeing and health of unpaid carers who support someone living with
dementia while working on projects that focused on understanding of unmet social care needs of people living with
dementia and their unpaid carers (2019–to present). This has given me an incredible opportunity to build
relationships with carers, health and adult social care professionals who support them and representatives from
the voluntary sector across England. It has also enabled me to identify that there is a need for research that would
specifically focus on physical and mental wellbeing of unpaid carers who support someone living with dementia.
As a result of this, I successfully applied for an NIHR Three Schools: Dementia Research Programme Award
(£32,166; Personal Career Award; July 2022-January 2023). This award was crucial in co-producing this
Fellowship as it allowed me to build a research team with eight unpaid carers of people living with dementia (co-
researchers). Between January and June 2023, we met online every month for two hours and worked together on
what the focus of the Fellowship should be. In the second half of t

criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision focused on unpaid carers' wellbeing and a distinct career trajectory towards leadership (g.1.a, g.1.b). The training plan is exceptionally detailed, covering specific skill gaps like conflict resolution and Behaviour Change Wheel, directly linked to project needs (g.1.c). Strong mentorship is evidenced by four supervisors with complementary expertise and structured meeting schedules (g.1.d).
criteria.drawbacks: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive ideas' could be further emphasized against the broader landscape of dementia research, though the co-production element provides strong differentiation.
criteria.rationale: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive ideas' could be further emphasized against the broader landscape of dementia research, though the co-production element provides strong differentiation.

Please describe your research career to date
I am a psychologist who works in adult social care research with particular interest in family members and friends
who support someone living with dementia (unpaid carers). There are two key messages from unpaid carers that
inspired this Fellowship: ‘carers are never asked what they needed/what they want’ and ‘you can’t pour from an

I have an MSc (research) in Clinical and Health Psychology, MSc in Psychology and PhD in Medical Sciences. I
have ten years’ experience in coordinating and successfully executing various research projects. I have acquired
experience in all stages of the research process – from protocol development, ethics, conduct of studies of
different designs (both quantitative and qualitative) through to dissemination of the results. Since 2014, I have built
a research profile in Health, Social Care and Wellbeing. To date,

he year, we continued to meet monthly to
discuss all work packages proposed in this Fellowship, dissemination activities and their role in the Fellowship if
awarded. Alongside this Fellowship application, we also co-produced a blog[1] and we engaged with an illustrator
who captured our work on how unpaid carers of people living with dementia can address some of their concerns
when it comes to their own health and wellbeing. In addition, our team also applied together with a local artist for
SHAPE Involve and Engage grant from the British Academy (£8000, submitted in June 2023, unsuccessful) to
enable us to hold a mixed-media workshop with unpaid carers of people living with dementia followed by an
exhibition to increase awareness of what it means to be a carer for a person with dementia. We are also working
together on academic paper and a podcast episode (for Dementia Researcher) to reflect on our experience of co-
producing this Fellowship application.
To conclude, this NIHR Advanced Fellowship represents an incredible opportunity for a step change in my career
trajectory – a crucial step of establishing myself as a future leader in dementia research. Namely, over the course
of t

he Fellowship I would like to start to generate knowledge on how we can support unpaid carers of people living
with dementia to look after their own health and wellbeing. Importantly, I would like for this knowledge to be co-
produced together with unpaid carers. This will ensure that the research I will be leading on will be meaningful to
unpaid carers, driven by their own needs. Ultimately as a researcher, I would like to make the needs of unpaid
carers visible to academic community, present and future health and social care professionals as well as policy
makers.

Proposed training and development programme
1. Training focused on behaviour change interventions: The proposed research is informed by
Behaviour Change Wheel. In 2022-2023 I attended two courses in Behaviour Change Wheel: Behaviour
Change Interventions: Introductory Principles and Practice (introductory) and Applied Principles and
Practice of Behaviour Change (intermediate) to support the development of this application. During the
Fellowship I would like to gain more in depth-knowledge of Behaviour Change Wheel and therefore I
propose to attend two Advanced Modules in Behaviour Change: Principles and Practice of Behavioural
Systems Mapping and the Behaviour Change Intervention Ontology (BCIO): How to use the BCIO for
different research and applied purposes. I plan to attend these courses during the first year of the
Fellowship. These courses are delivered by the developers of the model based at Centre for Behaviour
Change, UCL. In addition to gaining a specific knowledge, attending these courses will also allow me to
connect to other researchers interested in this specific approach. Importantly, Behaviour Change Wheel
represents one of the approaches to behaviour change. There ar
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
e other approaches to developing
interventions e.g. Intervention Mapping which provides a framework for developing theory-based and
evidence-based interventions applied to health promotion and disease prevention. To be able, in the
future, to support other researchers aiming to improve the wellbeing and health of unpaid carers of people
with dementia, I also propose to attend a 5-day summer course on Intervention Mapping: Designing
Theory-based and Evidence-based Programs at Maastricht University in the Netherlands. Being trained in
two key approaches to behaviour change intervention development will enable me to work beyond this
Fellowship on a range of projects and connect with people who apply these approaches in their work. I
plan to attend this course in the second year of the Fellowship.
2. Training in Co-production: as a researcher I am committed to work in a co-produced way together with
unpaid carers of people living with dementia, other academics, health and social care professionals. While
co-producing this application over the period of twelve months together with unpaid carers I have identified
two key areas where I need additional training: 1. working with conflict an

criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly states the Fellowship is a 'crucial step' to establish independence and leadership, clearly articulating why this award is the right next step (g.2.a, g.2.b). The proposal details how the award will build on existing experience by enabling new skills in Implementation Science and Economic Evaluation (g.2.c). Partnerships with NHS, social care, and third-sector organizations are well-developed (g.2.d).
criteria.drawbacks: The specific long-term impact of these new partnerships on the applicant's career beyond the Fellowship duration could be slightly more elaborated, though the current evidence is strong.
criteria.rationale: The specific long-term impact of these new partnerships on the applicant's career beyond the Fellowship duration could be slightly more elaborated, though the current evidence is strong.

6. Software skills: I would like to become an expert on two software packages I regularly use in my
research and will be also central to delivery of this Fellowship: NVivo and Qualtrics. As such I suggest
attending two courses: NVivo Expert Certification: this will enable me to be an in-house NVivo expert and
advisor for other researchers across University of Kent. I will also do Qualtrics online courses, again
allowing me to raise my profile in the research community.
7. Specific skills and knowledge needed for the next stage of the project (after Fellowship):
 Implementation Science Training: designed to build capacity and prepare for
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
implementation in particular
settings; third year of the Fellowship.
 Economic Evaluation Online: An introductory course; third year of the Fellowship.
8. Conferences: Conferences are an incredible opportunity to present one’s own research, to learn about up
to date evidence and make important connections. As such I plan to attend at least one conference
between years 2-3 of the Fellowship. I will focus on conferences in three areas: dementia; health
psychology/behaviour change; and ageing.

Name Dr Rosie Ashworth
Institution NHS Scotland Neuroprogressive and Dementia Network
Position The Lead for the Patient and Public Involvement group
Description of support provided
Rosie leads a group known as ‘Partners in Research’ that includes people living with dementia and their unpaid
carers. They work together on a range of activities including book writing, developing research proposals,
reviewing research materials and raising awareness. Rosie is also a Research Fellow at the University of
Edinburgh with a special interest in co-produced research.
Rosie played a central role in development of this Fellowship application as she provided me with advice on

ort me with this. We will
meet regularly once per two months to discuss the progress of the Fellowship. She will also be involved in the
outputs, and she will be member of SC.
Importantly, Sarah will also support me to think about my career beyond this Fellowship. She is based at Kent and
will mentor me in advancing my own career here and beyond.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 3
Name Professor Naji Tabet
Institution Brighton & Sussex Medical School
Position Professor, Dementia and Old Age Psychiatry
Description of support provided
Naji is the Director of the Centre for Dementia Studies (CDS) and the Course Leader of the MSc Dementia Studies
at Brighton and Sussex Medical School. He also leads the Dementia Theme at the NIHR Applied Research
Collaboration Kent, Surrey and Sussex. He is also the Dementia Speciality Co-Lead for NIHR Clinical Research
Network Kent, Surrey and Sussex.
Naji has led the unit on several important phase II-IV clinical trials trying to advance the treatment of Alzheimer’s
disease. Importantly he is also involved in studies that investigate the role of non-pharmacological interventions,
lifestyles and co-morbid physical illnesses in disease progression. He has

Please describe your research career to date
I am a psychologist who works in adult social care research with particular interest in family members and friends
who support someone living with dementia (unpaid carers). There are two key messages from unpaid carers that
inspired this Fellowship: ‘carers are never asked what they needed/what they want’ and ‘you can’t pour from an
empty jug.’ As such, I see this NIHR Advanced Fellowship as a beginning of my long-term commitment to help
unpaid carers to have good overall health and wellbeing.
I have an MSc (research) in Clinical and Health Psychology, MSc in Psychology and PhD in Medical Sciences. I
have ten years’ experience in coordinating and successfully executing various research projects. I have acquired
experience in all stages of the research process – from protocol development, ethics, conduct of studies of
different designs (both quantitative and qualitative) through to dissemination of the results. Since 2014, I have built
a research profile in Health, Social Care and Wellbeing. To date,

I have co-authored 33 publications (12 as a first
author and 11 as a second author) in peer-reviewed academic journals. According to ResearchGate, I have been
cited 538 times, with an h-index 13. I worked as a researcher in the Netherlands, the USA and in England. I
presented the research at international conferences focusing on health psychology, public health and adult social
care. Importantly, I also ensure that outcomes of my research reach diverse audiences, including talks at carers’
organisations and blogs focusing on unpaid carers.
My early postdoctoral career focused on developing and evaluating behavioural change interventions. For
example, as a Career Development Fellow at University of Cambridge (2014-2016), I tried to understand the
impact of communicating risk estimates on physicians' and patients' behaviour, wellbeing, and other relevant
clinical outcomes. I used range of study designs including randomised controlled trial (RCT), qualitative interviews
and focus groups as well as systematic reviews. Specifically, I was leading INFORM trial (Chief Investigator:
Professor Simon Griffin), open labelled, parallel group RCT of cardiovascular disease risk communication. An

I have co-authored 33 publications (12 as a first
author and 11 as a second author) in peer-reviewed academic journals. According to ResearchGate, I have been
cited 538 times, with an h-index 13. I worked as a researcher in the Netherlands, the USA and in England. I
presented the research at international conferences focusing on health psychology, public health and adult social
care. Importantly, I also ensure that outcomes of my research reach diverse audiences, including talks at carers’
organisations and blogs focusing on unpaid carers.
My early postdoctoral career focused on developing and evaluating behavioural change interventions. For
example, as a Career Development Fellow at University of Cambridge (2014-2016), I tried to understand the
impact of communicating risk estimates on physicians' and patients' behaviour, wellbeing, and other relevant
clinical outcomes. I used range of study designs including randomised controlled trial (RCT), qualitative interviews
and focus groups as well as systematic reviews. Specifically, I was leading INFORM trial (Chief Investigator:
Professor Simon Griffin), open labelled, parallel group RCT of cardiovascular disease risk communication. An

integral part of INFORM was a personalised web-based lifestyle programme to prevent cardiovascular disease
that I adapted for UK environment from American Heart to Health study. This has resulted in several papers with
me being a first author on two key papers: the paper reporting the results from the RCT and protocol paper.
I expanded what I learned from cardiovascular disease prevention on dementia prevention during my time as a
Research Fellow at University of Exeter (2017-2019). For example, as part of a wider project, I secured ESRC
Impact Acceleration Account Business Boost funding (approx. £10,000) for the MOVE study to allow us to initiate
collaboration with a mobile health application developer (based in Denmark) and to establish partnerships with
representatives of key stakeholders (e.g. patients, health care professionals, commissioners and policy makers).
During this time, I also became an Early Career Representative as part of International Research Network on
Dementia Prevention. This resulted in an editorial (in Aging & Mental Health) co-authored with five international
researchers highlighting that the career development of early- and mid-career researchers in deme

who support someone living with dementia (unpaid carers). There are two key messages from unpaid carers that

empty jug.’ As such, I see this NIHR Advanced Fellowship as a beginning of my long-term commitment to help

have ten years’ experience in coordinating and successfully executing various research projects. I have acquired

I started to be interested in a topic of wellbeing and health of unpaid carers who support someone living with
dementia while working on projects that focused on understanding of unmet social care needs of people living with
dementia and their unpaid carers (2019–to present). This has given me an incredible opportunity to build
relationships with carers, health and adult social care professionals who support them and representatives from
the voluntary sector across England. It has also enabled me to identify that there is a need for research that would
specifically focus on physical and mental wellbeing of unpaid carers who support someone living with dementia.
As a result of this, I successfully applied for an NIHR Three Schools: Dementia Research Programme Award
(£32,166; Personal Career Award; July 2022-January 2023). This award was crucial in co-producing this
Fellowship as it allowed me to build a research team with eight unpaid carers of people living with dementia (co-
researchers). Between January and June 2023, we met online every month for two hours and worked together on
what the focus of the Fellowship should be. In the second half of t

sub_criteria.name: Quality of the Plain English Summary
sub_criteria.definition: Clear, simple description of the research that a non-specialist audience can understand; explains problem, importance, plan, beneficiaries, and real-world use; avoids jargon; aligns with the detailed proposal.

signals.signal_text: Readability scores (e.g., Flesch-Kincaid)
Low density of unexplained jargon or technical terms
Presence of problem statement, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and real-world impact
Alignment with detailed proposal content
Sentence structure and coherence for non-specialist understanding
Emphasis on societal or patient relevance

sub_criteria.pros: The Plain English Summary demonstrates excellent readability with a Flesch-Kincaid grade of 7.36 and 0% long sentences (secpena__001). It clearly articulates the problem, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and real-world impact using accessible language (secpesor__001_a, secpesor__001_c). The low jargon density (2.35%) and strong alignment with the detailed proposal further support a high score.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the summary is strong, the lexical overlap with the detailed research plan is relatively low (0.154), which may indicate some divergence in terminology, though the content coverage remains comprehensive.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the summary is strong, the lexical overlap with the detailed research plan is relatively low (0.154), which may indicate some divergence in terminology, though the content coverage remains comprehensive.

evidence.text:
Aim(s) of the research: The focus of this Fellowship is on family members and friends who support someone
living with dementia (unpaid carers). Eight former and current unpaid carers highlighted that they would like to
understand how they can increase their physical activity, improve their diet and manage their time better.
Therefore, as a first step, we will prioritise which concern out of these three is the most important for unpaid carers
living across England. As a second step, we will then design an intervention that will help unpaid carers to address
the concern selected in the first step. As a last step, we will test whether the developed intervention is practical
and acceptable for unpaid carers and for those who may be delivering it.
Background to the research: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends who support a
person living with dementia. In the academic literature they are referred to as carers, unpaid carers, family carers
or supporters etc. but the family members and friends may not use any of these terms to describe themselves. As
a result of their caring responsibilities, carers are often unable to do things that would support their own health a
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
nd
wellbeing. Unpaid carers themselves highlighted that ‘It is exhausting to be a carer, it takes so much out of you’.
Currently, we know very little about how we can support unpaid carers to increase their physical activity, improve
their diet and manage their time better.
Design and methods used: In this Fellowship we will follow a step by step guide called the Behaviour Change
Wheel to understand how we can support unpaid carers to improve their health and wellbeing. In Stage 1 we will
conduct a needs assessment (through reviewing existing evidence and collecting information among 100 unpaid
carers living in England) to identify whether we should focus on e.g. helping carers to manage their time better (to
have ‘me time’). In Stage 2 we will use a range of methods to design the intervention itself. This will include a
consensus exercise with 30 unpaid carers to define the exact focus of the intervention. We will then conduct five
focus groups (3-5 people per group) with unpaid carers to gain further insight into how the intervention should look.
We will then design the intervention itself and share it with five focus groups (3-5 people per group) including e.g.
carers, health an

d social care professionals etc. to gather their feedback. In Stage 3 we will test whether the
proposed intervention is practical and acceptable by the unpaid carers and the people who may deliver it.
Patient/service user, carer and public involvement: Unpaid carers played a central role in co-producing this
Fellowship application. Over a period of twelve months we met on a monthly basis to decide together the focus of
the Fellowship, how we are going to answer our research questions, disseminate results etc. We were a team of
ten people - eight people self-identified as a former or current unpaid carer living across England and Scotland.
They will continue to work on the Fellowship as co-researchers – this means they will be part of the core research
team, they will jointly own the research and be responsible for it.
Dissemination: Unpaid carers highlighted that it is important that the results from this Fellowship reach a wide
range of people including carers, health and social care professionals and that the results are shared throughout
the Fellowship regularly. We will use a range of approaches, for example: Podcast/YouTube channel; articles; and
working with an illustrator to

evidence.text:
Research question: What does a feasible and acceptable intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or
time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia look like?
Background: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends of people living with dementia who
are unpaid carers. While being a carer can be rewarding, caring is associated with a range of mental and/or
physical health difficulties. Based on the evidence gap in the literature and working together with eight current and
former unpaid carers, we identified a need to develop an intervention to improve unpaid carers’ physical activity,
diet and time management in order to support their health and wellbeing.
Aims and objectives: The ultimate goal of this Fellowship is to design and evaluate feasibility of a behaviour
change intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living
with dementia.
Methods: The study will use mixed methods across three stages of work. Eight unpaid carers co-produced the
suggested research and will be part of the core research team throughout the Fellowship. In Stage 1 (needs
assessment) we will conduct a survey

ial care professionals etc. to gather feedback. In Stage 3 we will conduct a feasibility study to test whether
the proposed intervention is feasible and acceptable for unpaid carers and those who may deliver it.
Timelines for delivery: Stage 1: 1-12 months; Stage 2: 13-29 months; Stage 3: 30-44 months.
Anticipated impact and dissemination: A functional, feasible, and acceptable intervention targeting physical
activity, diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia across England. For
dissemination of results, we will use a range of approaches, for example: Podcast/YouTube channel; webinars;
articles for the Alzheimer's & Dementia Journal or the Journal of Dementia Care; working with an illustrator to
capture some of the findings etc.

sub_criteria.name: Evidence Review Quality & Relevance
sub_criteria.definition: How comprehensively and critically the application reviews existing literature and evidence.

signals.signal_text: Number and relevance of references cited
Presence of critical analysis vs. mere summary
Identification of knowledge gaps
Coherence and logical flow

sub_criteria.pros: The application explicitly identifies significant knowledge gaps regarding physical activity, diet, and time management for unpaid carers, citing weak existing evidence (secdrp__001_d, secdrp__001_e). The evidence review is logically structured with 58 transition phrases and a clear flow from context to budget (secafa__001). The proposal plans a systematic review to address these gaps (secsa__001_b).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the gaps are well-identified, the specific number of references cited in the evidence review section is not explicitly quantified in the provided text, though the quality of the gap identification is high.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the gaps are well-identified, the specific number of references cited in the evidence review section is not explicitly quantified in the provided text, though the quality of the gap identification is high.

evidence.text:
Research question: What does a feasible and acceptable intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or
time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia look like?
Background: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends of people living with dementia who
are unpaid carers. While being a carer can be rewarding, caring is associated with a range of mental and/or
physical health difficulties. Based on the evidence gap in the literature and working together with eight current and
former unpaid carers, we identified a need to develop an intervention to improve unpaid carers’ physical activity,
diet and time management in order to support their health and wellbeing.
Aims and objectives: The ultimate goal of this Fellowship is to design and evaluate feasibility of a behaviour
change intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living
with dementia.
Methods: The study will use mixed methods across three stages of work. Eight unpaid carers co-produced the
suggested research and will be part of the core research team throughout the Fellowship. In Stage 1 (needs
assessment) we will conduct a survey

(online or paper) with 100 unpaid carers of people living with dementia
across England to identify whether the focus of the intervention should be on physical activity and diet or time
management. This decision will also be supported by a systematic review to understand where the highest need
for an intervention development is. In Stage 2 we will develop the intervention itself focusing on key concern(s) as
identified in Stage 1, e.g. diet and physical activity. We will use a range of methods as recommended by the
Behaviour Change Wheel. First, we will conduct an online Delphi consensus exercise with 30 unpaid carers to
define target behaviours (e.g. healthy snacking; healthy cooking etc.). We will then conduct five focus groups (3-5
people per group) with unpaid carers to define the exact focus of the intervention including what needs to change
in the person and/or the environment in order to e.g. stop snacking on high-fat/salt/sugar snacks. Once we
understand what needs to change, the research team with the support of other experts will design the intervention
itself and conduct five focus groups (3-5 people per group) with e.g. unpaid carers, other family members, health
and soc

tely needed more support to protect their wellbeing’[6].
Review of existing evidence: how does the existing literature support this proposal?
Unpaid carers of people living with dementia experience a range of mental and physical health issues (e.g.
(e.g.[14-16]). Very little is known about physical activity, time management and diet of unpaid carers of
people with dementia when compared to other concerns (e.g. copying with emotions and stress[7]; knowledge
[8]; copying with terminal illness/grief[9] an getting help e.g. from adult social care services[10]).
No studies have explored how unpaid carers of people living with dementia can improve their time management.
The need to manage time better was identified by co-researchers themselves. Examples included: ‘I never get any
time for myself’; ‘I have no time to do shopping today’ and ‘I am so bad at managing my time’.
No studies have explored how unpaid carers of people living with dementia can improve their diet. Again, the need
to improve diet was identified by co-researchers. Examples included: neglecting own meals while focusing on
person with dementia; unhealthy snacking; poor quality ready meals; and binge eating.
A systematic

sub_criteria.definition: Whether the project scope matches the scale and expectations of the proposed award (doctoral, postdoctoral, etc.).

signals.signal_text: Alignment of aims with award level
Feasibility indicators (time, complexity, tasks)
Proportion of work relative to award expectations

sub_criteria.pros: The project scope is highly appropriate for a 44-month Fellowship, featuring a feasible three-stage design with specific sample sizes (100 carers, 30 consensus) and clear milestones (secpesor__001_b, secdrp__001_e). The Detailed Research Plan is substantial (5076 words), indicating adequate depth and proportionality to the award expectations (secafa__001).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks identified; the scope is well-matched to the award duration and complexity.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks identified; the scope is well-matched to the award duration and complexity.

Research question: What does a feasible and acceptable intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or
time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia look like?
Background: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends of people living with dementia who
are unpaid carers. While being a carer can be rewarding, caring is associated with a range of mental and/or
physical health difficulties. Based on the evidence gap in the literature and working together with eight current and
former unpaid carers, we identified a need to develop an intervention to improve unpaid carers’ physical activity,
diet and time management in order to support their health and wellbeing.
Aims and objectives: The ultimate goal of this Fellowship is to design and evaluate feasibility of a behaviour
change intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living
with dementia.
Methods: The study will use mixed methods across three stages of work. Eight unpaid carers co-produced the
suggested research and will be part of the core research team throughout the Fellowship. In Stage 1 (needs
assessment) we will conduct a survey

(online or paper) with 100 unpaid carers of people living with dementia
across England to identify whether the focus of the intervention should be on physical activity and diet or time
management. This decision will also be supported by a systematic review to understand where the highest need
for an intervention development is. In Stage 2 we will develop the intervention itself focusing on key concern(s) as
identified in Stage 1, e.g. diet and physical activity. We will use a range of methods as recommended by the
Behaviour Change Wheel. First, we will conduct an online Delphi consensus exercise with 30 unpaid carers to
define target behaviours (e.g. healthy snacking; healthy cooking etc.). We will then conduct five focus groups (3-5
people per group) with unpaid carers to define the exact focus of the intervention including what needs to change
in the person and/or the environment in order to e.g. stop snacking on high-fat/salt/sugar snacks. Once we
understand what needs to change, the research team with the support of other experts will design the intervention
itself and conduct five focus groups (3-5 people per group) with e.g. unpaid carers, other family members, health
and soc

ial care professionals etc. to gather feedback. In Stage 3 we will conduct a feasibility study to test whether
the proposed intervention is feasible and acceptable for unpaid carers and those who may deliver it.
Timelines for delivery: Stage 1: 1-12 months; Stage 2: 13-29 months; Stage 3: 30-44 months.
Anticipated impact and dissemination: A functional, feasible, and acceptable intervention targeting physical
activity, diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia across England. For
dissemination of results, we will use a range of approaches, for example: Podcast/YouTube channel; webinars;
articles for the Alzheimer's & Dementia Journal or the Journal of Dementia Care; working with an illustrator to
capture some of the findings etc.

sub_criteria.name: Research Design & Methodological Rigor
sub_criteria.definition: Clarity, appropriateness, and robustness of study design to answer the research questions.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of methods
Logical alignment between aims and methods
Discussion of controls, sample size, or methodology justification
Structured methodological sections

sub_criteria.pros: The research design is robust, explicitly detailing mixed methods including survey, systematic review, Delphi exercises, and focus groups aligned with the Behaviour Change Wheel (secpesor__001_b, secsa__001_b). The Detailed Research Plan provides specific analysis plans and methodological justifications (secdrp__001_e, secdrp__001_n).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the methods are well-described, specific details on control groups or power calculations for the survey component are not explicitly detailed in the provided snippets, though the overall design is rigorous.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the methods are well-described, specific details on control groups or power calculations for the survey component are not explicitly detailed in the provided snippets, though the overall design is rigorous.

were too methodologically weak to allow robust conclusions[19]. Methodological issues of the included studies
were also reported in another systematic review of exercise for caregiver–care recipient dyads[20].
What is the research question/aims and objectives?
The ultimate goal of this Fellowship is to design and evaluate the feasibility of a behaviour change intervention
targeting physical activity, diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia. Throughout
the Fellowship I will answer the following research question: What does a feasible and acceptable intervention
targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia in
England look like?
Project Plan
The proposed work is informed by Medical Research Council guidance for developing and evaluating complex
interventions[21]; the Behaviour Change Wheel[22]; and Co-production.

sub_criteria.name: Potential Impact on Patients, Public, and Health Services
sub_criteria.definition: Likely benefits or influence of the research on patients, carers, or health and care systems.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of direct or indirect patient/public benefits
Alignment with health priorities or societal needs
Clarity and specificity of expected outcomes

sub_criteria.pros: The application highlights direct benefits for unpaid carers' health and wellbeing and plans diverse dissemination strategies including documentaries, blogs, and policy engagement (secpesor__001_a, secdrp__001_w). It addresses high societal costs of dementia care, aligning with health priorities (secdrp__001_c).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the impact pathway is clear and well-supported by specific dissemination activities.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the impact pathway is clear and well-supported by specific dissemination activities.

we can support them to improve both their physical activity and diet or to manage their time better.
Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or wellbeing of the public and/or to
patients/service users, carers and health and care services?
The total costs of dementia in the UK in 2019 amounted to £34.7 billion. Unpaid care account for 40% (£13.9
billion) of the total costs[11]. Without unpaid carers it would be financially impossible for health and adult social
care to provide the huge amount of unpaid care (e.g. up to 100 hours per week during pandemic[12]) provided by
carers[11].
While caring for someone living with dementia can be rewarding[13], unpaid carers of people living with dementia
experience a range of mental and physical health issues(e.g.[14-16]) including stress, depression, anxiety, grief
and physical and emotional burden. Unpaid carers have an increased mortality[17] and worse physical and mental
health outcomes especially among women, those looking after a spouse, and those providing very high levels of
care[18]. An Alzheimer’s Society survey in 2020 found that two-thirds of unpaid carers of people living with
dementia ‘despera

sub_criteria.definition: Evidence of meaningful involvement of patients, carers, or communities.

signals.signal_text: Use of co-design, participatory methods, or consultations
References to ethical considerations and feedback mechanisms
Description of involvement at multiple project stages

The application includes a dedicated Patient & Public Involvement section (767 words) with significant integration into resource planning (secafa__001).

evidence.text:
Research question: What does a feasible and acceptable intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or
time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia look like?
Background: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends of people living with dementia who
are unpaid carers. While being a carer can be rewarding, caring is associated with a range of mental and/or
physical health difficulties. Based on the evidence gap in the literature and working together with eight current and
former unpaid carers, we identified a need to develop an intervention to improve unpaid carers’ physical activity,
diet and time management in order to support their health and wellbeing.
Aims and objectives: The ultimate goal of this Fellowship is to design and evaluate feasibility of a behaviour
change intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living
with dementia.
Methods: The study will use mixed methods across three stages of work. Eight unpaid carers co-produced the
suggested research and will be part of the core research team throughout the Fellowship. In Stage 1 (needs
assessment) we will conduct a survey

(online or paper) with 100 unpaid carers of people living with dementia
across England to identify whether the focus of the intervention should be on physical activity and diet or time
management. This decision will also be supported by a systematic review to understand where the highest need
for an intervention development is. In Stage 2 we will develop the intervention itself focusing on key concern(s) as
identified in Stage 1, e.g. diet and physical activity. We will use a range of methods as recommended by the
Behaviour Change Wheel. First, we will conduct an online Delphi consensus exercise with 30 unpaid carers to
define target behaviours (e.g. healthy snacking; healthy cooking etc.). We will then conduct five focus groups (3-5
people per group) with unpaid carers to define the exact focus of the intervention including what needs to change
in the person and/or the environment in order to e.g. stop snacking on high-fat/salt/sugar snacks. Once we
understand what needs to change, the research team with the support of other experts will design the intervention
itself and conduct five focus groups (3-5 people per group) with e.g. unpaid carers, other family members, health
and soc

eaking point’[6]. Importantly unpaid carers of people living with dementia highlighted while
working on this application that ‘Carers are overwhelmed as it is expected from them to do it all’ and ‘It is time to
recognise how the caring impacts family members and friends of those living with dementia’.
Between January 2023 and January 2024, I met monthly with eight unpaid former or current carers (co-
researchers) of people living with dementia who live across Scotland and England. They identified 22 health and
wellbeing concerns experienced by carers of people living with dementia, the most important ones being:
1.Exercise, 2. Diet, 3. Coping with stress, 4. Coping with terminal illness, 5. Coping with emotions, 6. Getting help,
7. Knowledge and 8. Managing their time better[1].
At the moment, very little is known about physical activity, time management and diet of unpaid carers of
people with dementia when compared to other concerns (copying with emotions and stress[7]; knowledge[8];
copying with terminal illness/grief[9] and getting help e.g. from adult social care services[10]).
Therefore, the focus of this Fellowship is on unpaid carers of people living with dementia in Englan

e people’s needs’. Therefore, there is a need to conduct a survey among
wider group of unpaid carers to identify the top health and wellbeing concern.
Survey: all study documents including survey will be developed together with eight co-researchers. The survey will
include questions about:
1. The Caregiving situation: former vs current carer, whether the carer and person with dementia live in the
same household, relationship to a person living with dementia, estimated hours of care per week, any
additional support received from other family members and friends, health care and adult social care
services etc.
2. Sociodemographic characteristics: age; gender; ethnicity; region; urban vs rural area; socioeconomic
status; English as a second language.
3. Carer quality of life of someone with dementia: a condition-specific measure of the quality of carers of
people with dementia[24].
4. Mode of data collection: paper or online.
5. Top health and wellbeing concern: able to choose from the three health and wellbeing concerns
suggested by co-researchers as described earlier. Participants will have an opportunity to suggest other
health and wellbeing concerns and will be provided also with

Within this Fellowship application I have requested funding for support staff (research associate) to the equivalent
of 50% FTE for a period of 44 months. We will also identify together any training needs (£3000 was requested to
support them). They will be part of project management group and attend SC meetings.
Project Management Group (PMG) will meet monthly to ensure the project is on track and discuss project related
issues. The group will include myself, support staff and eight co-researchers. Each member of the PMG will have
an opportunity to chair these meetings. In addition to attending monthly meetings, co-researchers expressed
interest in conducting the research itself including conducting focus groups, reviewing all study documents and
supporting dissemination activities. We agreed with co-researchers that on top of monthly meetings they may
contribute up to 10 hours per month to the research delivery. The type of activities and amount of commitment will
be decided with each co-researcher depending on their availability at that time as their situation and health can
fluctuate. Appropriate training will be provided and was incl

uded in the budget.
The SC will meet online at key stages of the project. The support staff, co-researchers and mentors will also
attend all SC meetings. SC will include academics with relevant expertise, health and social care professionals,
representatives from independent charities and commissioning officers.
The Brighton and Sussex Clinical Trials Unit (CTU): will support me in three areas: data management, study
management and statistical advice.
Ethics: before the start of the study, a favourable opinion will be sought from the Health Research Authority and/or
University of Kent. When needed Research Governance approvals will be sought from participating local
authorities and ADASS (Directors of Adult Social Care).
Success Criteria
The Fellowship will be a success if I develop feasible and acceptable intervention targeting physical activity and
diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia. The ongoing success criteria would be to
deliver each stage on time, recruit as intended and deliver outputs as planned.
Barriers/risks include the following:
 Failure to start on time due to delays in contract, ethical approval or recruitment. Risk low, allowing

I have co-authored 33 publications (12 as a first
author and 11 as a second author) in peer-reviewed academic journals. According to ResearchGate, I have been
cited 538 times, with an h-index 13. I worked as a researcher in the Netherlands, the USA and in England. I
presented the research at international conferences focusing on health psychology, public health and adult social
care. Importantly, I also ensure that outcomes of my research reach diverse audiences, including talks at carers’
organisations and blogs focusing on unpaid carers.
My early postdoctoral career focused on developing and evaluating behavioural change interventions. For
example, as a Career Development Fellow at University of Cambridge (2014-2016), I tried to understand the
impact of communicating risk estimates on physicians' and patients' behaviour, wellbeing, and other relevant
clinical outcomes. I used range of study designs including randomised controlled trial (RCT), qualitative interviews
and focus groups as well as systematic reviews. Specifically, I was leading INFORM trial (Chief Investigator:
Professor Simon Griffin), open labelled, parallel group RCT of cardiovascular disease risk communication. An

integral part of INFORM was a personalised web-based lifestyle programme to prevent cardiovascular disease
that I adapted for UK environment from American Heart to Health study. This has resulted in several papers with
me being a first author on two key papers: the paper reporting the results from the RCT and protocol paper.
I expanded what I learned from cardiovascular disease prevention on dementia prevention during my time as a
Research Fellow at University of Exeter (2017-2019). For example, as part of a wider project, I secured ESRC
Impact Acceleration Account Business Boost funding (approx. £10,000) for the MOVE study to allow us to initiate
collaboration with a mobile health application developer (based in Denmark) and to establish partnerships with
representatives of key stakeholders (e.g. patients, health care professionals, commissioners and policy makers).
During this time, I also became an Early Career Representative as part of International Research Network on
Dementia Prevention. This resulted in an editorial (in Aging & Mental Health) co-authored with five international
researchers highlighting that the career development of early- and mid-career researchers in deme

change intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living
with dementia.

suggested research and will be part of the core research team throughout the Fellowship. In Stage 1 (needs
assessment) we will conduct a survey

(online or paper) with 100 unpaid carers of people living with dementia
across England to identify whether the focus of the intervention should be on physical activity and diet or time
management. This decision will also be supported by a systematic review to understand where the highest need
for an intervention development is. In Stage 2 we will develop the intervention itself focusing on key concern(s) as

Behaviour Change Wheel. First, we will conduct an online Delphi consensus exercise with 30 unpaid carers to

understand what needs to change, the research team with the support of other experts will design the intervention
itself and conduct five focus groups (3-5 people per group) with e.g. unpaid carers, other family members, health
and soc

hs)
3. Stage 3 (feasibility study; 30-44 months).
Equality, diversity and inclusion (EDI): information will be collected and results will be summarised where
possible based on unpaid carer’s ethnicity/race, age, gender and sexual orientation. We will ensure that we will
recruit participants from a range of backgrounds including recruiting participants from specific areas to sample
different ethnicities, socioeconomic backgrounds and levels of access to health and dementia care services. We
will also engage with organisations that specifically support LGBTQ+ communities. I am also a member of
Dementia Community EDI specialty interest group and will draw on the members’ expertise.
Examples of the organisations that we will engage with are: Opening Doors; Switchboard Dementia; LGBTQ+
Dementia Advisory Group team; The Ethnic Minorities in Canterbury (EMIC); Diversity Resource International; the
West Sussex Alzheimer’s Society (link established with chair of the Dementia Strategy Minorities Sub-group),
Carers Support West Sussex (link with Bi-lingual Dementia Navigator); and Meri Yaadain CiC (supporting ethnic
communities). I will also draw on the links of the Clinical Research Network

criteria.name: Quality of the Plain English Summary
criteria.definition: Clear, simple description of the research that a non-specialist audience can understand; explains problem, importance, plan, beneficiaries, and real-world use; avoids jargon; aligns with the detailed proposal.

signals.signal_text: Readability scores (e.g., Flesch-Kincaid)
Low density of unexplained jargon or technical terms
Presence of problem statement, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and real-world impact
Alignment with detailed proposal content
Sentence structure and coherence for non-specialist understanding
Emphasis on societal or patient relevance

criteria.pros: The Plain English Summary demonstrates excellent readability with a Flesch-Kincaid grade of 7.36 and 0% long sentences (secpena__001). It clearly articulates the problem, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and real-world impact using accessible language (secpesor__001_a, secpesor__001_c). The low jargon density (2.35%) and strong alignment with the detailed proposal further support a high score.
criteria.drawbacks: While the summary is strong, the lexical overlap with the detailed research plan is relatively low (0.154), which may indicate some divergence in terminology, though the content coverage remains comprehensive.
criteria.rationale: While the summary is strong, the lexical overlap with the detailed research plan is relatively low (0.154), which may indicate some divergence in terminology, though the content coverage remains comprehensive.

nd
wellbeing. Unpaid carers themselves highlighted that ‘It is exhausting to be a carer, it takes so much out of you’.
Currently, we know very little about how we can support unpaid carers to increase their physical activity, improve
their diet and manage their time better.
Design and methods used: In this Fellowship we will follow a step by step guide called the Behaviour Change
Wheel to understand how we can support unpaid carers to improve their health and wellbeing. In Stage 1 we will
conduct a needs assessment (through reviewing existing evidence and collecting information among 100 unpaid

focus groups (3-5 people per group) with unpaid carers to gain further insight into how the intervention should look.

Research question: What does a feasible and acceptable intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or
time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia look like?
Background: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends of people living with dementia who
are unpaid carers. While being a carer can be rewarding, caring is associated with a range of mental and/or
physical health difficulties. Based on the evidence gap in the literature and working together with eight current and
former unpaid carers, we identified a need to develop an intervention to improve unpaid carers’ physical activity,
diet and time management in order to support their health and wellbeing.
Aims and objectives: The ultimate goal of this Fellowship is to design and evaluate feasibility of a behaviour
change intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living
with dementia.
Methods: The study will use mixed methods across three stages of work. Eight unpaid carers co-produced the
suggested research and will be part of the core research team throughout the Fellowship. In Stage 1 (needs
assessment) we will conduct a survey

ial care professionals etc. to gather feedback. In Stage 3 we will conduct a feasibility study to test whether
the proposed intervention is feasible and acceptable for unpaid carers and those who may deliver it.
Timelines for delivery: Stage 1: 1-12 months; Stage 2: 13-29 months; Stage 3: 30-44 months.
Anticipated impact and dissemination: A functional, feasible, and acceptable intervention targeting physical
activity, diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia across England. For
dissemination of results, we will use a range of approaches, for example: Podcast/YouTube channel; webinars;
articles for the Alzheimer's & Dementia Journal or the Journal of Dementia Care; working with an illustrator to
capture some of the findings etc.

criteria.name: Evidence Review Quality & Relevance
criteria.definition: How comprehensively and critically the application reviews existing literature and evidence.

signals.signal_text: Number and relevance of references cited
Presence of critical analysis vs. mere summary
Identification of knowledge gaps
Coherence and logical flow

criteria.pros: The application explicitly identifies significant knowledge gaps regarding physical activity, diet, and time management for unpaid carers, citing weak existing evidence (secdrp__001_d, secdrp__001_e). The evidence review is logically structured with 58 transition phrases and a clear flow from context to budget (secafa__001). The proposal plans a systematic review to address these gaps (secsa__001_b).
criteria.drawbacks: While the gaps are well-identified, the specific number of references cited in the evidence review section is not explicitly quantified in the provided text, though the quality of the gap identification is high.
criteria.rationale: While the gaps are well-identified, the specific number of references cited in the evidence review section is not explicitly quantified in the provided text, though the quality of the gap identification is high.

understand how they can increase their physical activity, improve their diet and manage their time better.
Therefore, as a first step, we will prioritise which concern out of these three is the most important for unpaid carers
living across England. As a second step, we will then design an intervention that will help unpaid carers to address
the concern selected in the first step. As a last step, we will test whether the developed intervention is practical
and acceptable for unpaid carers and for those who may be delivering it.

evidence.text:
Research question: What does a feasible and acceptable intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or
time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia look like?
Background: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends of people living with dementia who
are unpaid carers. While being a carer can be rewarding, caring is associated with a range of mental and/or
physical health difficulties. Based on the evidence gap in the literature and working together with eight current and
former unpaid carers, we identified a need to develop an intervention to improve unpaid carers’ physical activity,
diet and time management in order to support their health and wellbeing.
Aims and objectives: The ultimate goal of this Fellowship is to design and evaluate feasibility of a behaviour
change intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living
with dementia.
Methods: The study will use mixed methods across three stages of work. Eight unpaid carers co-produced the
suggested research and will be part of the core research team throughout the Fellowship. In Stage 1 (needs
assessment) we will conduct a survey

(online or paper) with 100 unpaid carers of people living with dementia
across England to identify whether the focus of the intervention should be on physical activity and diet or time
management. This decision will also be supported by a systematic review to understand where the highest need
for an intervention development is. In Stage 2 we will develop the intervention itself focusing on key concern(s) as
identified in Stage 1, e.g. diet and physical activity. We will use a range of methods as recommended by the
Behaviour Change Wheel. First, we will conduct an online Delphi consensus exercise with 30 unpaid carers to
define target behaviours (e.g. healthy snacking; healthy cooking etc.). We will then conduct five focus groups (3-5
people per group) with unpaid carers to define the exact focus of the intervention including what needs to change
in the person and/or the environment in order to e.g. stop snacking on high-fat/salt/sugar snacks. Once we
understand what needs to change, the research team with the support of other experts will design the intervention
itself and conduct five focus groups (3-5 people per group) with e.g. unpaid carers, other family members, health
and soc

tely needed more support to protect their wellbeing’[6].
Review of existing evidence: how does the existing literature support this proposal?
Unpaid carers of people living with dementia experience a range of mental and physical health issues (e.g.
(e.g.[14-16]). Very little is known about physical activity, time management and diet of unpaid carers of
people with dementia when compared to other concerns (e.g. copying with emotions and stress[7]; knowledge
[8]; copying with terminal illness/grief[9] an getting help e.g. from adult social care services[10]).
No studies have explored how unpaid carers of people living with dementia can improve their time management.
The need to manage time better was identified by co-researchers themselves. Examples included: ‘I never get any
time for myself’; ‘I have no time to do shopping today’ and ‘I am so bad at managing my time’.
No studies have explored how unpaid carers of people living with dementia can improve their diet. Again, the need
to improve diet was identified by co-researchers. Examples included: neglecting own meals while focusing on
person with dementia; unhealthy snacking; poor quality ready meals; and binge eating.
