evidence.text:
Research question: What does a feasible and acceptable intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or
time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia look like?
Background: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends of people living with dementia who
are unpaid carers. While being a carer can be rewarding, caring is associated with a range of mental and/or
physical health difficulties. Based on the evidence gap in the literature and working together with eight current and
former unpaid carers, we identified a need to develop an intervention to improve unpaid carers’ physical activity,
diet and time management in order to support their health and wellbeing.
Aims and objectives: The ultimate goal of this Fellowship is to design and evaluate feasibility of a behaviour
change intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living
with dementia.
Methods: The study will use mixed methods across three stages of work. Eight unpaid carers co-produced the
suggested research and will be part of the core research team throughout the Fellowship. In Stage 1 (needs
assessment) we will conduct a survey

(online or paper) with 100 unpaid carers of people living with dementia
across England to identify whether the focus of the intervention should be on physical activity and diet or time
management. This decision will also be supported by a systematic review to understand where the highest need
for an intervention development is. In Stage 2 we will develop the intervention itself focusing on key concern(s) as
identified in Stage 1, e.g. diet and physical activity. We will use a range of methods as recommended by the
Behaviour Change Wheel. First, we will conduct an online Delphi consensus exercise with 30 unpaid carers to
define target behaviours (e.g. healthy snacking; healthy cooking etc.). We will then conduct five focus groups (3-5
people per group) with unpaid carers to define the exact focus of the intervention including what needs to change
in the person and/or the environment in order to e.g. stop snacking on high-fat/salt/sugar snacks. Once we
understand what needs to change, the research team with the support of other experts will design the intervention
itself and conduct five focus groups (3-5 people per group) with e.g. unpaid carers, other family members, health
and soc

ial care professionals etc. to gather feedback. In Stage 3 we will conduct a feasibility study to test whether
the proposed intervention is feasible and acceptable for unpaid carers and those who may deliver it.
Timelines for delivery: Stage 1: 1-12 months; Stage 2: 13-29 months; Stage 3: 30-44 months.
Anticipated impact and dissemination: A functional, feasible, and acceptable intervention targeting physical
activity, diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia across England. For
dissemination of results, we will use a range of approaches, for example: Podcast/YouTube channel; webinars;
articles for the Alzheimer's & Dementia Journal or the Journal of Dementia Care; working with an illustrator to
capture some of the findings etc.

Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or wellbeing of the public and/or to
patients/service users, carers and health and care services?
The total costs of dementia in the UK in 2019 amounted to £34.7 billion. Unpaid care account for 40% (£13.9
billion) of the total costs[11]. Without unpaid carers it would be financially impossible for health and adult social
care to provide the huge amount of unpaid care (e.g. up to 100 hours per week during pandemic[12]) provided by
carers[11].
While caring for someone living with dementia can be rewarding[13], unpaid carers of people living with dementia
experience a range of mental and physical health issues(e.g.[14-16]) including stress, depression, anxiety, grief
and physical and emotional burden. Unpaid carers have an increased mortality[17] and worse physical and mental
health outcomes especially among women, those looking after a spouse, and those providing very high levels of
care[18]. An Alzheimer’s Society survey in 2020 found that two-thirds of unpaid carers of people living with
dementia ‘despera

eaking point’[6]. Importantly unpaid carers of people living with dementia highlighted while
working on this application that ‘Carers are overwhelmed as it is expected from them to do it all’ and ‘It is time to
recognise how the caring impacts family members and friends of those living with dementia’.
Between January 2023 and January 2024, I met monthly with eight unpaid former or current carers (co-
researchers) of people living with dementia who live across Scotland and England. They identified 22 health and
wellbeing concerns experienced by carers of people living with dementia, the most important ones being:
1.Exercise, 2. Diet, 3. Coping with stress, 4. Coping with terminal illness, 5. Coping with emotions, 6. Getting help,
7. Knowledge and 8. Managing their time better[1].
At the moment, very little is known about physical activity, time management and diet of unpaid carers of
people with dementia when compared to other concerns (copying with emotions and stress[7]; knowledge[8];
copying with terminal illness/grief[9] and getting help e.g. from adult social care services[10]).
Therefore, the focus of this Fellowship is on unpaid carers of people living with dementia in Englan

e people’s needs’. Therefore, there is a need to conduct a survey among
wider group of unpaid carers to identify the top health and wellbeing concern.
Survey: all study documents including survey will be developed together with eight co-researchers. The survey will
include questions about:
1. The Caregiving situation: former vs current carer, whether the carer and person with dementia live in the
same household, relationship to a person living with dementia, estimated hours of care per week, any
additional support received from other family members and friends, health care and adult social care
services etc.
2. Sociodemographic characteristics: age; gender; ethnicity; region; urban vs rural area; socioeconomic
status; English as a second language.
3. Carer quality of life of someone with dementia: a condition-specific measure of the quality of carers of
people with dementia[24].
4. Mode of data collection: paper or online.
5. Top health and wellbeing concern: able to choose from the three health and wellbeing concerns
suggested by co-researchers as described earlier. Participants will have an opportunity to suggest other

our publication and dissemination strategy.
Within this Fellowship application I have requested funding for support staff (research associate) to the equivalent
of 50% FTE for a period of 44 months. We will also identify together any training needs (£3000 was requested to
support them). They will be part of project management group and attend SC meetings.
Project Management Group (PMG) will meet monthly to ensure the project is on track and discuss project related
issues. The group will include myself, support staff and eight co-researchers. Each member of the PMG will have
an opportunity to chair these meetings. In addition to attending monthly meetings, co-researchers expressed
interest in conducting the research itself including conducting focus groups, reviewing all study documents and
supporting dissemination activities. We agreed with co-researchers that on top of monthly meetings they may
contribute up to 10 hours per month to the research delivery. The type of activities and amount of commitment will
be decided with each co-researcher depending on their availability at that time as their situation and health can
fluctuate. Appropriate training will be provided and was incl

uded in the budget.
The SC will meet online at key stages of the project. The support staff, co-researchers and mentors will also
attend all SC meetings. SC will include academics with relevant expertise, health and social care professionals,
representatives from independent charities and commissioning officers.
The Brighton and Sussex Clinical Trials Unit (CTU): will support me in three areas: data management, study
management and statistical advice.
Ethics: before the start of the study, a favourable opinion will be sought from the Health Research Authority and/or
University of Kent. When needed Research Governance approvals will be sought from participating local
authorities and ADASS (Directors of Adult Social Care).
Success Criteria
The Fellowship will be a success if I develop feasible and acceptable intervention targeting physical activity and
diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia. The ongoing success criteria would be to
deliver each stage on time, recruit as intended and deliver outputs as planned.
Barriers/risks include the following:
 Failure to start on time due to delays in contract, ethical approval or recruitment. Risk low, allowing

I have co-authored 33 publications (12 as a first
author and 11 as a second author) in peer-reviewed academic journals. According to ResearchGate, I have been
cited 538 times, with an h-index 13. I worked as a researcher in the Netherlands, the USA and in England. I
presented the research at international conferences focusing on health psychology, public health and adult social
care. Importantly, I also ensure that outcomes of my research reach diverse audiences, including talks at carers’
organisations and blogs focusing on unpaid carers.
My early postdoctoral career focused on developing and evaluating behavioural change interventions. For
example, as a Career Development Fellow at University of Cambridge (2014-2016), I tried to understand the
impact of communicating risk estimates on physicians' and patients' behaviour, wellbeing, and other relevant
clinical outcomes. I used range of study designs including randomised controlled trial (RCT), qualitative interviews
and focus groups as well as systematic reviews. Specifically, I was leading INFORM trial (Chief Investigator:
Professor Simon Griffin), open labelled, parallel group RCT of cardiovascular disease risk communication. An

integral part of INFORM was a personalised web-based lifestyle programme to prevent cardiovascular disease
that I adapted for UK environment from American Heart to Health study. This has resulted in several papers with
me being a first author on two key papers: the paper reporting the results from the RCT and protocol paper.
I expanded what I learned from cardiovascular disease prevention on dementia prevention during my time as a
Research Fellow at University of Exeter (2017-2019). For example, as part of a wider project, I secured ESRC
Impact Acceleration Account Business Boost funding (approx. £10,000) for the MOVE study to allow us to initiate
collaboration with a mobile health application developer (based in Denmark) and to establish partnerships with
representatives of key stakeholders (e.g. patients, health care professionals, commissioners and policy makers).
During this time, I also became an Early Career Representative as part of International Research Network on
Dementia Prevention. This resulted in an editorial (in Aging & Mental Health) co-authored with five international
researchers highlighting that the career development of early- and mid-career researchers in deme

evidence.text:
Aim(s) of the research: The focus of this Fellowship is on family members and friends who support someone
living with dementia (unpaid carers). Eight former and current unpaid carers highlighted that they would like to
understand how they can increase their physical activity, improve their diet and manage their time better.
Therefore, as a first step, we will prioritise which concern out of these three is the most important for unpaid carers
living across England. As a second step, we will then design an intervention that will help unpaid carers to address
the concern selected in the first step. As a last step, we will test whether the developed intervention is practical
and acceptable for unpaid carers and for those who may be delivering it.
Background to the research: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends who support a
person living with dementia. In the academic literature they are referred to as carers, unpaid carers, family carers
or supporters etc. but the family members and friends may not use any of these terms to describe themselves. As
a result of their caring responsibilities, carers are often unable to do things that would support their own health a
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
d social care professionals etc. to gather their feedback. In Stage 3 we will test whether the
proposed intervention is practical and acceptable by the unpaid carers and the people who may deliver it.
Patient/service user, carer and public involvement: Unpaid carers played a central role in co-producing this
Fellowship application. Over a period of twelve months we met on a monthly basis to decide together the focus of
the Fellowship, how we are going to answer our research questions, disseminate results etc. We were a team of
ten people - eight people self-identified as a former or current unpaid carer living across England and Scotland.
They will continue to work on the Fellowship as co-researchers – this means they will be part of the core research
team, they will jointly own the research and be responsible for it.
Dissemination: Unpaid carers highlighted that it is important that the results from this Fellowship reach a wide
range of people including carers, health and social care professionals and that the results are shared throughout
the Fellowship regularly. We will use a range of approaches, for example: Podcast/YouTube channel; articles; and
working with an illustrator to
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
Research question: What does a feasible and acceptable intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or
time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia look like?
Background: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends of people living with dementia who
are unpaid carers. While being a carer can be rewarding, caring is associated with a range of mental and/or
physical health difficulties. Based on the evidence gap in the literature and working together with eight current and
former unpaid carers, we identified a need to develop an intervention to improve unpaid carers’ physical activity,
diet and time management in order to support their health and wellbeing.
Aims and objectives: The ultimate goal of this Fellowship is to design and evaluate feasibility of a behaviour
change intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living
with dementia.
Methods: The study will use mixed methods across three stages of work. Eight unpaid carers co-produced the
suggested research and will be part of the core research team throughout the Fellowship. In Stage 1 (needs
assessment) we will conduct a survey

(online or paper) with 100 unpaid carers of people living with dementia
across England to identify whether the focus of the intervention should be on physical activity and diet or time
management. This decision will also be supported by a systematic review to understand where the highest need
for an intervention development is. In Stage 2 we will develop the intervention itself focusing on key concern(s) as
identified in Stage 1, e.g. diet and physical activity. We will use a range of methods as recommended by the
Behaviour Change Wheel. First, we will conduct an online Delphi consensus exercise with 30 unpaid carers to
define target behaviours (e.g. healthy snacking; healthy cooking etc.). We will then conduct five focus groups (3-5
people per group) with unpaid carers to define the exact focus of the intervention including what needs to change
in the person and/or the environment in order to e.g. stop snacking on high-fat/salt/sugar snacks. Once we
understand what needs to change, the research team with the support of other experts will design the intervention
itself and conduct five focus groups (3-5 people per group) with e.g. unpaid carers, other family members, health
and soc

hs)
3. Stage 3 (feasibility study; 30-44 months).
Equality, diversity and inclusion (EDI): information will be collected and results will be summarised where
possible based on unpaid carer’s ethnicity/race, age, gender and sexual orientation. We will ensure that we will
recruit participants from a range of backgrounds including recruiting participants from specific areas to sample
different ethnicities, socioeconomic backgrounds and levels of access to health and dementia care services. We
will also engage with organisations that specifically support LGBTQ+ communities. I am also a member of
Dementia Community EDI specialty interest group and will draw on the members’ expertise.
Examples of the organisations that we will engage with are: Opening Doors; Switchboard Dementia; LGBTQ+
Dementia Advisory Group team; The Ethnic Minorities in Canterbury (EMIC); Diversity Resource International; the
West Sussex Alzheimer’s Society (link established with chair of the Dementia Strategy Minorities Sub-group),
Carers Support West Sussex (link with Bi-lingual Dementia Navigator); and Meri Yaadain CiC (supporting ethnic
communities). I will also draw on the links of the Clinical Research Network

sub_criteria.definition: How well-structured, clear, and relevant the training plan is in relation to the project’s goals and the applicant’s research needs.

signals.signal_text: Completeness and clarity of training description
Alignment of training topics with project methods or techniques
Structured timeline or sequencing of training activities
Explicit links between training content and skill acquisition

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides a highly detailed, structured training plan covering eight distinct areas (Behaviour Change, Co-production, EDI, Impact, Leadership, Software, Implementation Science, Conferences). Each area includes specific course titles, providers (e.g., UCL, Maastricht University, Gestalt Centre), and clear rationales linking the training to project methods and career goals. The plan is explicitly sequenced across the 44-month fellowship timeline (Year 1, 2, 3).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details identified; the training plan is comprehensive and fully evidenced.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details identified; the training plan is comprehensive and fully evidenced.

evidence.text:
Proposed training and development programme
1. Training focused on behaviour change interventions: The proposed research is informed by
Behaviour Change Wheel. In 2022-2023 I attended two courses in Behaviour Change Wheel: Behaviour
Change Interventions: Introductory Principles and Practice (introductory) and Applied Principles and
Practice of Behaviour Change (intermediate) to support the development of this application. During the
Fellowship I would like to gain more in depth-knowledge of Behaviour Change Wheel and therefore I
propose to attend two Advanced Modules in Behaviour Change: Principles and Practice of Behavioural
Systems Mapping and the Behaviour Change Intervention Ontology (BCIO): How to use the BCIO for
different research and applied purposes. I plan to attend these courses during the first year of the
Fellowship. These courses are delivered by the developers of the model based at Centre for Behaviour
Change, UCL. In addition to gaining a specific knowledge, attending these courses will also allow me to
connect to other researchers interested in this specific approach. Importantly, Behaviour Change Wheel
represents one of the approaches to behaviour change. There ar
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
e other approaches to developing
interventions e.g. Intervention Mapping which provides a framework for developing theory-based and
evidence-based interventions applied to health promotion and disease prevention. To be able, in the
future, to support other researchers aiming to improve the wellbeing and health of unpaid carers of people
with dementia, I also propose to attend a 5-day summer course on Intervention Mapping: Designing
Theory-based and Evidence-based Programs at Maastricht University in the Netherlands. Being trained in
two key approaches to behaviour change intervention development will enable me to work beyond this
Fellowship on a range of projects and connect with people who apply these approaches in their work. I
plan to attend this course in the second year of the Fellowship.
2. Training in Co-production: as a researcher I am committed to work in a co-produced way together with
unpaid carers of people living with dementia, other academics, health and social care professionals. While
co-producing this application over the period of twelve months together with unpaid carers I have identified
two key areas where I need additional training: 1. working with conflict an
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
d confrontation in teams; and 2.
facilitation skills. As such I plan to attend three courses delivered by Gestalt Centre in London during the
first year of Fellowship: Group Facilitation: Key concepts, skills and personal development (one day in
person workshop); Working with Conflict and Confrontation in Groups and Teams (two days in person
workshop); and Group Facilitation Training Intensive (nine days in total spread over four months).
3. Equality, Diversity and Inclusion (EDI): As a researcher, I am aware that the research I conduct mainly
reflects experiences of those who self-identify as e.g. white, British, heterosexual and from middle class.
As such in this Fellowship, I intend to ensure that we will take proactive steps to recruit people from
diverse backgrounds – this includes co-researchers, members of SC and research participants. While as
an EDI representative at University of Kent I have opportunities to attend a range of training sessions, in
this Fellowship I would like to specifically attend training sessions related to EDI issues when conducting
research. Therefore, I plan to attend following training throughout the Fellowship: a) Improving the
inclusion of under-
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
served groups in health research (online webinar by INVOLVE); and b) An Ethical
Perspective on Implementing Equal Opportunities in PPI (online webinar). Importantly as part of the NIHR
Applied Research Collaboration I am member of a working group of researchers interested in the inclusion
of individuals living with dementia and their carers in research.
4. Impact and dissemination training: I aim to become a researcher who has an influence beyond
academia. Specifically, I would like to make the needs of unpaid carers visible to present and future health
and social care professionals as well as policy makers. I plan to attend the following courses: a) Impact
Training for Researchers (online course provided by Fast Track Impact over 5 weeks; first year of the
Fellowship) and Compelling Communication Skills (6 week course delivered by University of Cambridge;
second year of the Fellowship). In addition, I will apply for the Royal Society Pairing Scheme. Each year
30 research scientists are paired with UK parliamentarians and civil servants. They learn about each
other’s work by spending time together in Westminster and the researcher’s institutions. Those taking part
gain an insight i
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
nto how research findings can help inform policy making and come away with a better
understanding of how they can get involved.
5. Leadership training: I would describe a good leader in three words: kind; adaptive and self-aware. I had
an opportunity to attend the NIHR Virtual Leadership Programme for social care researchers which was a
scaled down version of NIHR Future-Focused Leadership Programme. As the content of that course
reflected my own values, I would like to attend the NIHR Future-Focused Leadership Programme as part
of this Fellowship.
6. Software skills: I would like to become an expert on two software packages I regularly use in my
research and will be also central to delivery of this Fellowship: NVivo and Qualtrics. As such I suggest
attending two courses: NVivo Expert Certification: this will enable me to be an in-house NVivo expert and
advisor for other researchers across University of Kent. I will also do Qualtrics online courses, again
allowing me to raise my profile in the research community.
7. Specific skills and knowledge needed for the next stage of the project (after Fellowship):
 Implementation Science Training: designed to build capacity and prepare for

Position Senior Research Fellow
Description of support provided
Sarah is a Senior Research Fellow and BPS Chartered Psychologist. She is an expert in behavioural health
psychology, with a specific focus on areas of nutrition (breastfeeding, obesity and malnutrition), sanitation and
training of healthcare professionals. Her current and previous research has focused on three UN Sustainable
Development Goals: Zero hunger; Good health and wellbeing (i.e. to decrease the risk of non-communicable
diseases); and Clean water and sanitation.
Sarah has an extensive expertise in supervising projects with a focus on behavioural science approaches to
design, delivery and evaluation of health behaviour change interventions. She is also interested in Implementation
Science approaches to evaluation and how evidence can be translated into policy and practice.
Sarah will provide me with expertise during the Fellowship on the design of the health behaviour change
intervention as well as the feasibility study. Importantly, Implementation Science is an area where I lack an
experience and this is the area where I would like to gain more knowledge. Sarah will supp
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
Proposed training and development programme
1. Training focused on behaviour change interventions: The proposed research is informed by
Behaviour Change Wheel. In 2022-2023 I attended two courses in Behaviour Change Wheel: Behaviour
Change Interventions: Introductory Principles and Practice (introductory) and Applied Principles and
Practice of Behaviour Change (intermediate) to support the development of this application. During the
Fellowship I would like to gain more in depth-knowledge of Behaviour Change Wheel and therefore I
propose to attend two Advanced Modules in Behaviour Change: Principles and Practice of Behavioural
Systems Mapping and the Behaviour Change Intervention Ontology (BCIO): How to use the BCIO for
different research and applied purposes. I plan to attend these courses during the first year of the
Fellowship. These courses are delivered by the developers of the model based at Centre for Behaviour
Change, UCL. In addition to gaining a specific knowledge, attending these courses will also allow me to
connect to other researchers interested in this specific approach. Importantly, Behaviour Change Wheel
represents one of the approaches to behaviour change. There ar
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
e other approaches to developing
interventions e.g. Intervention Mapping which provides a framework for developing theory-based and
evidence-based interventions applied to health promotion and disease prevention. To be able, in the
future, to support other researchers aiming to improve the wellbeing and health of unpaid carers of people
with dementia, I also propose to attend a 5-day summer course on Intervention Mapping: Designing
Theory-based and Evidence-based Programs at Maastricht University in the Netherlands. Being trained in
two key approaches to behaviour change intervention development will enable me to work beyond this
Fellowship on a range of projects and connect with people who apply these approaches in their work. I
plan to attend this course in the second year of the Fellowship.
2. Training in Co-production: as a researcher I am committed to work in a co-produced way together with
unpaid carers of people living with dementia, other academics, health and social care professionals. While

two key areas where I need additional training: 1. working with conflict an
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

d confrontation in teams; and 2.
facilitation skills. As such I plan to attend three courses delivered by Gestalt Centre in London during the
first year of Fellowship: Group Facilitation: Key concepts, skills and personal development (one day in
person workshop); Working with Conflict and Confrontation in Groups and Teams (two days in person
workshop); and Group Facilitation Training Intensive (nine days in total spread over four months).
3. Equality, Diversity and Inclusion (EDI): As a researcher, I am aware that the research I conduct mainly
reflects experiences of those who self-identify as e.g. white, British, heterosexual and from middle class.
As such in this Fellowship, I intend to ensure that we will take proactive steps to recruit people from
diverse backgrounds – this includes co-researchers, members of SC and research participants. While as
an EDI representative at University of Kent I have opportunities to attend a range of training sessions, in

research. Therefore, I plan to attend following training throughout the Fellowship: a) Improving the
inclusion of under-

of individuals living with dementia and their carers in research.

gain an insight i

evidence.text:
ces issues at local, national and international levels. CHSS builds the evidence base for policy and
practice to prevent ill-health, promote wellbeing and meet needs for care, focusing on primary and community
services, public health and social care working with academic collaborators across the UK and the world. CHSS
works with local government and health services across Kent, Surrey and Sussex to build research capacity and
culture and address the key health and social care research needs across the region.
CHSS has a substantial number of links with other organisations including National Institute of Health Research
(NIHR) Applied Research Collaboration Kent, Surrey and Sussex (ARC KSS) and the Kent Adult Social Care
Partnership. ARC KSS is the national lead for social care. Primary and Community Care and Living Well with
Dementia are also key themes of the ARC, with active and interlinked research programmes. There are well-
established relationships between the ARC theme leads (primary care lead is Professor Harm van Marwijk in
Brighton and Sussex Medical School and Professor Naji Tabet (my mentor) is theme lead for dementia). As an
employee of University of Kent I have an acc
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
entre for Health Services Studies (CHSS), within the Division of Law, Society and Social
Justice at the University of Kent, I am delighted to support Dr Barbora Silarova’s application for an NIHR
Advanced Fellowshipand confirm that we will ensure she is provided with all necessary support and be able to
draw on the wider services of the University in order to deliver the work in time and within budget. CHSS is a
dynamic research unit delivering high quality, generalisable, applied health and care research that addresses
specific health or care issues locally, regionally, nationally and internationally.
We provide a welcoming and supportive environment for Early and Mid-Career Researchers and are committed to
support her employment at CHSS. We have a strong track record of supporting the development of Early and Mid-
Career Researchers, many of whom have progressed their careers at CHSS and are now leading their own
research projects as independent researchers. CHSS is well placed to support her proposed research through the
NIHR Applied Research Collaboration Kent, Surrey and Sussex (ARC KSS) and the Kent Research Partnership,
which is one of six NIHR funded social care research ca
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
pacity partnerships in England.
Dr Silarova will be a full member of our staff team, currently 60 researchers, and will have opportunities to engage
in all the activities run by the Centre and the University for Research Staff. She will have dedicated office space
and equipment for completing her research. Two of her mentors: Professor Lindsay Forbes and Dr Sarah Hotham
are based at CHSS. They will support Dr Silarova with the delivery of the Fellowship itself but also with her career
beyond the Fellowship and help her to identify leadership opportunities across CHSS and at University of Kent.
Dr Silarova will benefit from being based in an active research environment, with a strong track record in health
and social care, including several completed and ongoing NIHR grants relating to the needs of and outcomes of
informal carers. Dr Silarova will also benefit from strong links with ARC KSS, the Personal Social Services
Research Unit (PSSRU) and the NIHR School for Social Care (of which Kent is a founding member). Through the
ARC KSS, she will have access to implementation, co-production and Public and Patient Involvement and
Engagement support and the researcher development program

evidence.text:
ntia should be a
global priority.
I started to be interested in a topic of wellbeing and health of unpaid carers who support someone living with
dementia while working on projects that focused on understanding of unmet social care needs of people living with
dementia and their unpaid carers (2019–to present). This has given me an incredible opportunity to build
relationships with carers, health and adult social care professionals who support them and representatives from
the voluntary sector across England. It has also enabled me to identify that there is a need for research that would
specifically focus on physical and mental wellbeing of unpaid carers who support someone living with dementia.
As a result of this, I successfully applied for an NIHR Three Schools: Dementia Research Programme Award
(£32,166; Personal Career Award; July 2022-January 2023). This award was crucial in co-producing this
Fellowship as it allowed me to build a research team with eight unpaid carers of people living with dementia (co-
researchers). Between January and June 2023, we met online every month for two hours and worked together on
what the focus of the Fellowship should be. In the second half of t

he year, we continued to meet monthly to
discuss all work packages proposed in this Fellowship, dissemination activities and their role in the Fellowship if
awarded. Alongside this Fellowship application, we also co-produced a blog[1] and we engaged with an illustrator
who captured our work on how unpaid carers of people living with dementia can address some of their concerns
when it comes to their own health and wellbeing. In addition, our team also applied together with a local artist for
SHAPE Involve and Engage grant from the British Academy (£8000, submitted in June 2023, unsuccessful) to
enable us to hold a mixed-media workshop with unpaid carers of people living with dementia followed by an
exhibition to increase awareness of what it means to be a carer for a person with dementia. We are also working
together on academic paper and a podcast episode (for Dementia Researcher) to reflect on our experience of co-
producing this Fellowship application.
To conclude, this NIHR Advanced Fellowship represents an incredible opportunity for a step change in my career
trajectory – a crucial step of establishing myself as a future leader in dementia research. Namely, over the course
of t

d social care professionals etc. to gather their feedback. In Stage 3 we will test whether the
proposed intervention is practical and acceptable by the unpaid carers and the people who may deliver it.
Patient/service user, carer and public involvement: Unpaid carers played a central role in co-producing this
Fellowship application. Over a period of twelve months we met on a monthly basis to decide together the focus of
the Fellowship, how we are going to answer our research questions, disseminate results etc. We were a team of
ten people - eight people self-identified as a former or current unpaid carer living across England and Scotland.
They will continue to work on the Fellowship as co-researchers – this means they will be part of the core research
team, they will jointly own the research and be responsible for it.
Dissemination: Unpaid carers highlighted that it is important that the results from this Fellowship reach a wide
range of people including carers, health and social care professionals and that the results are shared throughout
the Fellowship regularly. We will use a range of approaches, for example: Podcast/YouTube channel; articles; and
working with an illustrator to
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
Research question: What does a feasible and acceptable intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or
time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia look like?
Background: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends of people living with dementia who
are unpaid carers. While being a carer can be rewarding, caring is associated with a range of mental and/or
physical health difficulties. Based on the evidence gap in the literature and working together with eight current and
former unpaid carers, we identified a need to develop an intervention to improve unpaid carers’ physical activity,
diet and time management in order to support their health and wellbeing.
Aims and objectives: The ultimate goal of this Fellowship is to design and evaluate feasibility of a behaviour
change intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living
with dementia.
Methods: The study will use mixed methods across three stages of work. Eight unpaid carers co-produced the
suggested research and will be part of the core research team throughout the Fellowship. In Stage 1 (needs
assessment) we will conduct a survey

(online or paper) with 100 unpaid carers of people living with dementia
across England to identify whether the focus of the intervention should be on physical activity and diet or time
management. This decision will also be supported by a systematic review to understand where the highest need
for an intervention development is. In Stage 2 we will develop the intervention itself focusing on key concern(s) as
identified in Stage 1, e.g. diet and physical activity. We will use a range of methods as recommended by the
Behaviour Change Wheel. First, we will conduct an online Delphi consensus exercise with 30 unpaid carers to
define target behaviours (e.g. healthy snacking; healthy cooking etc.). We will then conduct five focus groups (3-5
people per group) with unpaid carers to define the exact focus of the intervention including what needs to change
in the person and/or the environment in order to e.g. stop snacking on high-fat/salt/sugar snacks. Once we
understand what needs to change, the research team with the support of other experts will design the intervention
itself and conduct five focus groups (3-5 people per group) with e.g. unpaid carers, other family members, health
and soc

While the PPI team is diverse, there is less explicit detail on the demographic composition of the academic staff beyond the lead applicant.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
sub_criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

sub_criteria.pros: A robust dissemination strategy is outlined, including a documentary, podcasts, blogs, and articles specifically designed to reach unpaid carers, health professionals, and policy makers. The plan explicitly links study objectives (improving carer wellbeing) to actionable outcomes like influencing policy and supporting health services.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the dissemination channels are diverse, the specific mechanisms for measuring the 'benefit' or 'impact' on the public (e.g., pre/post surveys of policy makers, usage metrics of the documentary) are not detailed in the provided text.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the dissemination channels are diverse, the specific mechanisms for measuring the 'benefit' or 'impact' on the public (e.g., pre/post surveys of policy makers, usage metrics of the documentary) are not detailed in the provided text.

evidence.text:
I have co-authored 33 publications (12 as a first
author and 11 as a second author) in peer-reviewed academic journals. According to ResearchGate, I have been
cited 538 times, with an h-index 13. I worked as a researcher in the Netherlands, the USA and in England. I
presented the research at international conferences focusing on health psychology, public health and adult social
care. Importantly, I also ensure that outcomes of my research reach diverse audiences, including talks at carers’
organisations and blogs focusing on unpaid carers.
My early postdoctoral career focused on developing and evaluating behavioural change interventions. For
example, as a Career Development Fellow at University of Cambridge (2014-2016), I tried to understand the
impact of communicating risk estimates on physicians' and patients' behaviour, wellbeing, and other relevant
clinical outcomes. I used range of study designs including randomised controlled trial (RCT), qualitative interviews
and focus groups as well as systematic reviews. Specifically, I was leading INFORM trial (Chief Investigator:
Professor Simon Griffin), open labelled, parallel group RCT of cardiovascular disease risk communication. An

he year, we continued to meet monthly to
discuss all work packages proposed in this Fellowship, dissemination activities and their role in the Fellowship if
awarded. Alongside this Fellowship application, we also co-produced a blog[1] and we engaged with an illustrator
who captured our work on how unpaid carers of people living with dementia can address some of their concerns
when it comes to their own health and wellbeing. In addition, our team also applied together with a local artist for
SHAPE Involve and Engage grant from the British Academy (£8000, submitted in June 2023, unsuccessful) to
enable us to hold a mixed-media workshop with unpaid carers of people living with dementia followed by an
exhibition to increase awareness of what it means to be a carer for a person with dementia. We are also working
together on academic paper and a podcast episode (for Dementia Researcher) to reflect on our experience of co-
producing this Fellowship application.
To conclude, this NIHR Advanced Fellowship represents an incredible opportunity for a step change in my career
trajectory – a crucial step of establishing myself as a future leader in dementia research. Namely, over the course

sub_criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
sub_criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

sub_criteria.pros: The application explicitly targets seldom-heard groups including ethnic minorities, LGBTQ+ communities, and those with language barriers. Recruitment strategies involve partnerships with specific third-sector organizations to reach these groups. Barriers such as language are addressed through document translation and interpreter services.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While strategies for language barriers are well-resourced (translation, interpreters), the specific mitigation strategies for other potential barriers (e.g., digital exclusion for online focus groups, time poverty for working carers) are less explicitly detailed in the recruitment section, though flexible modes are mentioned.
sub_criteria.rationale: While strategies for language barriers are well-resourced (translation, interpreters), the specific mitigation strategies for other potential barriers (e.g., digital exclusion for online focus groups, time poverty for working carers) are less explicitly detailed in the recruitment section, though flexible modes are mentioned.

sub_criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

sub_criteria.pros: Inclusivity is embedded across the entire lifecycle: co-researchers are involved in design, recruitment, and dissemination. Accessibility is ensured through translated materials, interpreter support, and flexible participation modes (online/in-person, paper/online surveys) to accommodate fluctuating health and time constraints.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The plan for 'ongoing evaluation and adaptation' (wp.5.c) regarding inclusivity is implied through the co-researcher feedback loops but lacks a specific, dedicated mechanism or timeline for formally reviewing and adapting inclusion strategies mid-project.
sub_criteria.rationale: The plan for 'ongoing evaluation and adaptation' (wp.5.c) regarding inclusivity is implied through the co-researcher feedback loops but lacks a specific, dedicated mechanism or timeline for formally reviewing and adapting inclusion strategies mid-project.

criteria.name: Integration of Lived Experience in Research Design
criteria.definition: How input from patients, carers, or people with relevant lived experience has shaped the research questions, design, and methods.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of consultation or co-design activities
Specific examples of changes made to study design based on lived experience
References to patient or community advisory groups

criteria.pros: The application demonstrates exceptional integration of lived experience. Eight unpaid carers co-produced the entire application, identified research concerns, and will act as co-researchers throughout the project (design, Delphi exercises, focus groups). Specific examples of design changes include the decision to use Zoom over MS Teams based on co-researcher feedback and the inclusion of flexible participation modes.
criteria.drawbacks: While the integration is deep, the specific 'changes made to study design' (wp.1.b) are described as general adaptations (e.g., platform choice, flexible timing) rather than fundamental shifts to the core research questions or methodology, though the co-production of the application itself is a strong indicator of design influence.
criteria.rationale: While the integration is deep, the specific 'changes made to study design' (wp.1.b) are described as general adaptations (e.g., platform choice, flexible timing) rather than fundamental shifts to the core research questions or methodology, though the co-production of the application itself is a strong indicator of design influence.

I started to be interested in a topic of wellbeing and health of unpaid carers who support someone living with
dementia while working on projects that focused on understanding of unmet social care needs of people living with
dementia and their unpaid carers (2019–to present). This has given me an incredible opportunity to build
relationships with carers, health and adult social care professionals who support them and representatives from
the voluntary sector across England. It has also enabled me to identify that there is a need for research that would
specifically focus on physical and mental wellbeing of unpaid carers who support someone living with dementia.
As a result of this, I successfully applied for an NIHR Three Schools: Dementia Research Programme Award
(£32,166; Personal Career Award; July 2022-January 2023). This award was crucial in co-producing this
Fellowship as it allowed me to build a research team with eight unpaid carers of people living with dementia (co-
researchers). Between January and June 2023, we met online every month for two hours and worked together on
what the focus of the Fellowship should be. In the second half of t

he year, we continued to meet monthly to
discuss all work packages proposed in this Fellowship, dissemination activities and their role in the Fellowship if
awarded. Alongside this Fellowship application, we also co-produced a blog[1] and we engaged with an illustrator
who captured our work on how unpaid carers of people living with dementia can address some of their concerns
when it comes to their own health and wellbeing. In addition, our team also applied together with a local artist for

together on academic paper and a podcast episode (for Dementia Researcher) to reflect on our experience of co-

evidence.text:
Research question: What does a feasible and acceptable intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or
time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia look like?
Background: Across the UK there are 700,000 family members and friends of people living with dementia who
are unpaid carers. While being a carer can be rewarding, caring is associated with a range of mental and/or
physical health difficulties. Based on the evidence gap in the literature and working together with eight current and
former unpaid carers, we identified a need to develop an intervention to improve unpaid carers’ physical activity,
diet and time management in order to support their health and wellbeing.
Aims and objectives: The ultimate goal of this Fellowship is to design and evaluate feasibility of a behaviour
change intervention targeting both physical activity and diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living
with dementia.
Methods: The study will use mixed methods across three stages of work. Eight unpaid carers co-produced the
suggested research and will be part of the core research team throughout the Fellowship. In Stage 1 (needs
assessment) we will conduct a survey

(online or paper) with 100 unpaid carers of people living with dementia
across England to identify whether the focus of the intervention should be on physical activity and diet or time
management. This decision will also be supported by a systematic review to understand where the highest need
for an intervention development is. In Stage 2 we will develop the intervention itself focusing on key concern(s) as
identified in Stage 1, e.g. diet and physical activity. We will use a range of methods as recommended by the
Behaviour Change Wheel. First, we will conduct an online Delphi consensus exercise with 30 unpaid carers to
define target behaviours (e.g. healthy snacking; healthy cooking etc.). We will then conduct five focus groups (3-5
people per group) with unpaid carers to define the exact focus of the intervention including what needs to change
in the person and/or the environment in order to e.g. stop snacking on high-fat/salt/sugar snacks. Once we
understand what needs to change, the research team with the support of other experts will design the intervention
itself and conduct five focus groups (3-5 people per group) with e.g. unpaid carers, other family members, health
and soc

criteria.name: Diversity and Inclusivity of the Research Team
criteria.definition: Extent to which the research team reflects diversity in terms of skills, experience, and demographic representation.

signals.signal_text: Explicit mentions of team composition across gender, ethnicity, age, or other relevant characteristics
Statements about inclusion in hiring or team-building
Evidence of equitable task allocation and collaboration

criteria.pros: The co-researcher team is explicitly described as diverse, including individuals from minority ethnic backgrounds, LGBTQ+ identities, and varying socioeconomic statuses. The plan includes recruiting an additional co-researcher from a minority background to further diversify voices, demonstrating a clear commitment to inclusive team composition.
criteria.drawbacks: The evidence focuses heavily on the diversity of the co-researcher team (PPI contributors) rather than the diversity of the academic research team itself. While the PPI team is diverse, there is less explicit detail on the demographic composition of the academic staff beyond the lead applicant.
criteria.rationale: The evidence focuses heavily on the diversity of the co-researcher team (PPI contributors) rather than the diversity of the academic research team itself. While the PPI team is diverse, there is less explicit detail on the demographic composition of the academic staff beyond the lead applicant.

criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

criteria.pros: A robust dissemination strategy is outlined, including a documentary, podcasts, blogs, and articles specifically designed to reach unpaid carers, health professionals, and policy makers. The plan explicitly links study objectives (improving carer wellbeing) to actionable outcomes like influencing policy and supporting health services.
criteria.drawbacks: While the dissemination channels are diverse, the specific mechanisms for measuring the 'benefit' or 'impact' on the public (e.g., pre/post surveys of policy makers, usage metrics of the documentary) are not detailed in the provided text.
criteria.rationale: While the dissemination channels are diverse, the specific mechanisms for measuring the 'benefit' or 'impact' on the public (e.g., pre/post surveys of policy makers, usage metrics of the documentary) are not detailed in the provided text.

evidence.text:
I have co-authored 33 publications (12 as a first
author and 11 as a second author) in peer-reviewed academic journals. According to ResearchGate, I have been
cited 538 times, with an h-index 13. I worked as a researcher in the Netherlands, the USA and in England. I
presented the research at international conferences focusing on health psychology, public health and adult social
care. Importantly, I also ensure that outcomes of my research reach diverse audiences, including talks at carers’
organisations and blogs focusing on unpaid carers.
My early postdoctoral career focused on developing and evaluating behavioural change interventions. For
example, as a Career Development Fellow at University of Cambridge (2014-2016), I tried to understand the
impact of communicating risk estimates on physicians' and patients' behaviour, wellbeing, and other relevant
clinical outcomes. I used range of study designs including randomised controlled trial (RCT), qualitative interviews
and focus groups as well as systematic reviews. Specifically, I was leading INFORM trial (Chief Investigator:
Professor Simon Griffin), open labelled, parallel group RCT of cardiovascular disease risk communication. An

he year, we continued to meet monthly to
discuss all work packages proposed in this Fellowship, dissemination activities and their role in the Fellowship if
awarded. Alongside this Fellowship application, we also co-produced a blog[1] and we engaged with an illustrator
who captured our work on how unpaid carers of people living with dementia can address some of their concerns
when it comes to their own health and wellbeing. In addition, our team also applied together with a local artist for
SHAPE Involve and Engage grant from the British Academy (£8000, submitted in June 2023, unsuccessful) to
enable us to hold a mixed-media workshop with unpaid carers of people living with dementia followed by an
exhibition to increase awareness of what it means to be a carer for a person with dementia. We are also working
together on academic paper and a podcast episode (for Dementia Researcher) to reflect on our experience of co-
producing this Fellowship application.
To conclude, this NIHR Advanced Fellowship represents an incredible opportunity for a step change in my career
trajectory – a crucial step of establishing myself as a future leader in dementia research. Namely, over the course
of t

team, they will jointly own the research and be responsible for it.
Dissemination: Unpaid carers highlighted that it is important that the results from this Fellowship reach a wide
range of people including carers, health and social care professionals and that the results are shared throughout
the Fellowship regularly. We will use a range of approaches, for example: Podcast/YouTube channel; articles; and
working with an illustrator to

ial care professionals etc. to gather feedback. In Stage 3 we will conduct a feasibility study to test whether
the proposed intervention is feasible and acceptable for unpaid carers and those who may deliver it.
Timelines for delivery: Stage 1: 1-12 months; Stage 2: 13-29 months; Stage 3: 30-44 months.
Anticipated impact and dissemination: A functional, feasible, and acceptable intervention targeting physical
activity, diet or time management of unpaid carers of people living with dementia across England. For
dissemination of results, we will use a range of approaches, for example: Podcast/YouTube channel; webinars;
articles for the Alzheimer's & Dementia Journal or the Journal of Dementia Care; working with an illustrator to
capture some of the findings etc.

criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

criteria.pros: The application explicitly targets seldom-heard groups including ethnic minorities, LGBTQ+ communities, and those with language barriers. Recruitment strategies involve partnerships with specific third-sector organizations to reach these groups. Barriers such as language are addressed through document translation and interpreter services.
criteria.drawbacks: While strategies for language barriers are well-resourced (translation, interpreters), the specific mitigation strategies for other potential barriers (e.g., digital exclusion for online focus groups, time poverty for working carers) are less explicitly detailed in the recruitment section, though flexible modes are mentioned.
criteria.rationale: While strategies for language barriers are well-resourced (translation, interpreters), the specific mitigation strategies for other potential barriers (e.g., digital exclusion for online focus groups, time poverty for working carers) are less explicitly detailed in the recruitment section, though flexible modes are mentioned.

recruit participants from a range of backgrounds including recruiting participants from specific areas to sample

Dementia Advisory Group team; The Ethnic Minorities in Canterbury (EMIC); Diversity Resource International; the
West Sussex Alzheimer’s Society (link established with chair of the Dementia Strategy Minorities Sub-group),

communities). I will also draw on the links of the Clinical Research Network

lead (Kent, Surrey and Sussex),
namely the Research Engagement Network that have connections with Somalis and South Sudanese
communities.

wish to participate in a focus group, e.g. due to a language barrier, an option will be provided to have an individual
interview with an interpreter present. All documents will be translated into three additional languages.

criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

criteria.pros: Inclusivity is embedded across the entire lifecycle: co-researchers are involved in design, recruitment, and dissemination. Accessibility is ensured through translated materials, interpreter support, and flexible participation modes (online/in-person, paper/online surveys) to accommodate fluctuating health and time constraints.
criteria.drawbacks: The plan for 'ongoing evaluation and adaptation' (wp.5.c) regarding inclusivity is implied through the co-researcher feedback loops but lacks a specific, dedicated mechanism or timeline for formally reviewing and adapting inclusion strategies mid-project.
criteria.rationale: The plan for 'ongoing evaluation and adaptation' (wp.5.c) regarding inclusivity is implied through the co-researcher feedback loops but lacks a specific, dedicated mechanism or timeline for formally reviewing and adapting inclusion strategies mid-project.

criteria.name: Costed and Resourced Plan for Research Inclusion
criteria.definition: Evidence that research inclusion has been properly budgeted and resourced within the proposal.

signals.signal_text: Explicit budget line(s) for inclusion activities (PPI, outreach, recruitment support)
Mention of staff time, materials, or training dedicated to inclusion
Alignment between inclusion plan and allocated resources

criteria.pros: The application provides a fully costed plan for inclusion. The budget explicitly includes lines for PPI attendance payments, carer replacement costs, travel, internet costs, document translation (up to 3 languages), and interpreter services. This demonstrates a strong alignment between the inclusion plan and allocated resources.
criteria.drawbacks: There are no significant drawbacks; the budgeting for inclusion is comprehensive and directly supports the stated inclusion strategies.
criteria.rationale: There are no significant drawbacks; the budgeting for inclusion is comprehensive and directly supports the stated inclusion strategies.

sub_criteria.name: Use of Formatting, Headings, and Subheadings
sub_criteria.definition: Effective use of text formatting to make the application clear, navigable, and easy to read.

signals.signal_text: Presence and consistency of headings and subheadings
Use of bold or emphasis for key terms
Structured hierarchy of sections (logical nesting of headings)
Patterns detected via text parsing for formatting markers

sub_criteria.pros: The application form analysis confirms a robust structural hierarchy with 32 numbered heading-like lines and 37 list markers, ensuring clear navigation. The presence of 13 extracted emphasis markers and 224 table-like budget lines demonstrates effective use of formatting to distinguish key terms and data. The section order follows a logical progression from applicant context to budget.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While 13 emphasis markers were detected, the analysis notes that bold/emphasis may be lost during text extraction; therefore, the score for emphasis usage is slightly reduced to account for potential parser limitations rather than absolute certainty of the original formatting.
sub_criteria.rationale: While 13 emphasis markers were detected, the analysis notes that bold/emphasis may be lost during text extraction; therefore, the score for emphasis usage is slightly reduced to account for potential parser limitations rather than absolute certainty of the original formatting.

sub_criteria.definition: Avoidance of unnecessary repetition within or across sections.

signals.signal_text: Semantic similarity measures between sentences/paragraphs
Detection of repeated phrases or ideas
Novelty score for each section (unique content vs. overlap)

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates excellent control over duplication with a very low duplicate sentence rate (0.017) and a low repeated line rate (0.102). Lexical overlap between distinct sections (e.g., Plain English Summary and Scientific Abstract at 0.376) indicates that content is tailored to specific audiences rather than simply copied, showing high novelty scores for each section.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While overlap rates are low, the presence of some lexical overlap (up to 0.376) between the Plain English Summary and Scientific Abstract is expected but prevents a perfect score for absolute uniqueness, as some core concepts must necessarily repeat across summaries.

Structured hierarchy of sections (logical nesting of headings)
Patterns detected via text parsing for formatting markers

The presence of 13 extracted emphasis markers and 224 table-like budget lines demonstrates effective use of formatting to distinguish key terms and data. The section order follows a logical progression from applicant context to budget.

criteria.name: Limited Duplication and Repetition
criteria.definition: Avoidance of unnecessary repetition within or across sections.

signals.signal_text: Semantic similarity measures between sentences/paragraphs
Detection of repeated phrases or ideas
Novelty score for each section (unique content vs. overlap)

criteria.pros: The application demonstrates excellent control over duplication with a very low duplicate sentence rate (0.017) and a low repeated line rate (0.102). Lexical overlap between distinct sections (e.g., Plain English Summary and Scientific Abstract at 0.376) indicates that content is tailored to specific audiences rather than simply copied, showing high novelty scores for each section.
criteria.drawbacks: While overlap rates are low, the presence of some lexical overlap (up to 0.376) between the Plain English Summary and Scientific Abstract is expected but prevents a perfect score for absolute uniqueness, as some core concepts must necessarily repeat across summaries.
